item.title: Zili Huang
item.description:
Zili Huang

PhD student, Population Health Sciences (Health Systems Track)
email zilihuang@fas.harvard.edu

Zili Huang is a PhD student in the Population Health Sciences Program, Health Systems Track. Her research focuses on healthcare management and organizational behavior, with the goal of improving the efficiency and effectiveness of health systems through an organizational perspective. She is particularly interested in how healthcare organizations adapt to policy changes, financial incentives, and market dynamics, as well as how these factors influence provider behavior and health outcomes. Zili holds a Bachelor of Management Sciences in Public Administration from Shanghai Jiao Tong University in China and a Master of Science in Global Health and Population from the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health.

item.title: Zifu Wang
item.description:
Zifu Wang

Postdoctoral Research Fellow, Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA)
email zifu_wang@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Google Scholar

Zifu Wang, PhD is a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA), whose research lies at the intersection of Geographic Information Science (GIS), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and computational science. His work focuses on transforming complex spatial and textual data into evidence-based intelligence for public-policy applications in humanitarian response, crime prevention, public health, and transportation safety.

item.title: Zichao Li
item.description:
Zichao Li

PhD candidate in Population Health Sciences, Chan School of Public Health
email zichaoli@g.harvard.edu

Zichao Li is a PhD candidate in Population Health Sciences at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. Her research focuses on how health communication, especially health misinformation, influences vaccine and cancer-related health outcomes. She uses multilevel modeling and computational methods, including machine learning, to uncover social patterns in health behavior and disparities. Her work bridges public health, behavioral science, and data-driven analysis to inform health interventions.

item.title: Zhongyu Wei
item.description:
Zhongyu Wei

PhD Student in Human Development, Learning and Teaching, HGSE
email zhongyu_wei@g.harvard.edu

Zhongyu Wei is a PhD student in the Human Development, Learning and Teaching program at Harvard University. She is interested in exploring how young children's curiosity and inquiry drive their language and cognitive development. She uses quantitative and computational methods to analyze observational data. Zhongyu holds a B.A. in linguistics and economics from Tsinghua University and an EdM in international education policy from the Harvard Graduate School of Education.

item.title: Zheng Ma
item.description:
Zheng Ma

PhD Student in Social Policy and Government, HKS
email zma@g.harvard.edu
Zheng Ma is a PhD student in Social Policy and Government at Harvard University. His research interests lie primarily in leveraging computational methods to understand social phenomena and solve social problems. He hope to explore more how data-driven approaches can reveal intricate social dynamics and inform policy decisions in various contexts. Prior to coming to Harvard, he obtained an M.A. in Social Sciences from the University of Chicago and a B.A. in Economics and Political Science from the University of Wisconsin-Madison.

Visiting Scholar, China Biographical Database

JD Candidate, Stanford Law School

Zehua’s research explores the intersection of law, AI, and local politics. He uses computational methods to identify policy interventions that foster grassroots civic participation and strengthen institutional capacity.

item.description:
Zachary Lim

PhD Candidate, Education
email zlim@g.harvard.edu
Zac investigates how researchers navigate methodological disputes by employing natural language processing techniques to analyze the meaning of contentious words in academic publications. He's also interested in using this approach to augment philosophical analyses of concepts.

item.title: Zach Wehrwein
item.description:
Zach Wehrwein

Sociology, GSAS
email wehrwein@fas.harvard.edu
My research interests are in social network analysis, causal inference, political sociology, and ecoNomic history. With IQSS support, I am currently working with a network scientist to create a very large dataset of social media usage during the 2016 election. I am also currently working on two smaller scale social network analysis projects, one concerning financial markets in the 17th century and aNother examining friendships in American high schools in the 1990s.

item.title: Yuyang Shi
item.description:
Yuyang Shi

IQSS Computer Lab Assistant
email yuyangshi@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Yuyang Shi's research centers around authoritarian politics and state-society relations. Specifically, his current project explores the concept of digital authoritarian resilience, examining how authoritarian regimes bolster their online propaganda efforts in the multimedia age.

item.title: Yujing Huang
item.description:
Yujing Huang

Department of Linguistics, GSAS
person
email yujinghuang@fas.harvard.edu
Yujing Huang is a doctoral student in the Department of Linguistics. She is interested in how different components of language interact. Her research interest includes how meaning is expressed via syntactic structure (that is, the syntax-semantics interface) and how sound structure interacts with this structure as well (i.e., the phoNology-syntax interface). Her approach to these questions is mainly experimental. She is currently working on an eye-tracking experiment with adults on the interpretation of verbal arguments. She is also working on a tonal dialect in China.

  • People By Last Name

    H - J

  • Role

    Affiliated Graduate Students

Original source: https://iq.harvard.edu/people/yujing-huang

item.title: Yuhao Wang
item.description:
Yuhao Wang

MA Candidate, Regional Studies East Asia Program, GSAS
email yuhaowang@fas.harvard.edu

Yuhao Wang is an MA candidate in the Regional Studies East Asia (RSEA) Program. He received his BA in Philosophy, Politics & Economics (PPE) from Renmin University. His research uses quantitative tools as well as data science techniques to analysis the interaction of China’s foreign and domestic politics as well as broader concerns in the international political economy. His recent study has been presented at the Asian Political Methodology Meeting, the MPSA, and the APSA annual meeting.

item.title: Yuhan Zhang
item.description:
Yuhan Zhang

PhD Student in Linguistics, GSAS
email yuz551@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Yuhan Zhang holds a BA in English Language and Literature from Tsinghua University, China, and is currently a doctoral student in the Department of Linguistics at Harvard, working with Kathryn Davidson. She is interested in using experimental and quantitative methods to understand semantics and pragmatics during communication and information interpretation. She also works on adult language processing by using the self-paced reading method to unravel the cognitive mechanisms underlying language comprehension and production. Her current experimental semantic project involves identifying contextual factors in conversations that cause referential opacity in belief reports. She is also using corpus linguistic methodologies to investigate the phonological correlation between stress shift and vowel reduction in English verb nominalization.

item.title: Yuan Pei
item.description:
Yuan Pei

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, GSAS
email yuanpei@g.harvard.edu

Yuan Pei is a doctoral student in Public Policy at Harvard. Yuan's research focuses on development economics and behavioral economics. His current research explores how changing social norms can affect people's decision to enter the civil service. Another project of his is studying the impact of distance learning on reducing urban-rural education gap in China. Prior to beginning his PhD, Yuan worked for two years at the University of Pennsylvania and received his BA from UC Berkeley.

item.description:
Yousra Neberai

PhD Candidate, Public Policy (Economics), GSAS
email yousraneberai@g.harvard.edu

Yousra is a PhD Candidate in the Public Policy program (Economics Track). She studies economic development, particularly related to the provision of public goods, infrastructure, and health. Prior to her PhD, she worked as a pre-doctoral research fellow at Yale and at NERA economic consulting. She graduated from Harvard College where she studied Government.

item.title: Yoonje (Jea) Lee
item.description:
Yoonje (Jea) Lee

PhD Student in Government, GSAS
email yoonjelee@g.harvard.edu

Yoonje (Jea) is a PhD Student in the department of Government at Harvard University. She is broadly interested in comparative political behavior, political economy of development, intergroup relations and migration in the Global South, particularly Southeast Asia. Methodologically, she is interested in causal inference and unstructured data.

item.title: Yoon Jin Lee
item.description:
Yoon Jin Lee

Government, GSAS
email yjlee@fas.harvard.edu
I am a PhD candidate in the Government Department focusing on international relations/foreign policy. My dissertation explores the British and South Korean legislators' dispositional and situational determinants of intuitive vs. deliberative foreign policy decision-making, specifically with regards to the UK/ROK's bilateral security relations with the United States. I use a variety of methods, including inductive discourse analysis, automated text analysis, and experiments.

item.title: Yoly Yue Liu
item.description:
Yoly Yue Liu

PhD Student in Education Policy, GSAS/HGSE
email yolyliu@g.harvard.edu

Yoly Yue Liu is a PhD student studying how education drives systemic change. Her research uses causal inference to identify effective education policies and practices in low- and middle-income countries. Through field experiments, she investigates how teaching practices can cultivate intrinsically motivated, interactive, and lasting learning; how communities can mobilize local resources to support learning and address local challenges; and how stakeholders can collaborate to foster a learning culture rooted in local realities. Before her PhD, Yoly worked as a policy researcher with United Nations agencies.

item.title: Yizhou Jin

GSAS + HBS, Economics

Yizhou is a PhD student in the Business Economics program at GSAS and HBS. His research interests span topics in labor ecoNomics, development ecoNomics and corporate finance. Yizhou holds a BA degree in Mathematics and Economics at UC Berkeley. He also worked for Citigroup, Houlihan Lokey, and SiNochem Petroleum before coming to Harvard.

item.title: Yinan Wang
item.description:
Yinan Wang

PhD Candidate in Sociology, GSAS
email yinanwang@g.harvard.edu

Yinan is a doctoral student in Sociology at Harvard University. Her research interests broadly focus on exploring how social institutions interact to create inequalities and stratifications along axes of gender, class, and race in the labor market using experiment methods. She received her BA in English and Sociology from Tsinghua University.

item.title: Yenee Soh
item.description:
Yenee Soh

Research Associate, Social and Behavioral Sciences, Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
email ysoh@hsph.harvard.edu
laptop_windows HSPH Profile

 

Yenee Soh, ScD, SM, is a social epidemiologist and research associate in the Department of Social and Behavioral Sciences at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. Dr. Soh earned her doctorate in Social and Behavioral Sciences and master’s in Epidemiology from the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. She also holds a bachelor’s degree in Mathematics from Macalester College. Previously, she was a post-doctoral research fellow at the Kaiser Permanente Northern California Division of Research.

item.title: Xin Wen
item.description:
Xin Wen

Inner Asian and Altaic Studies, GSAS
email xwen0113@gmail.com
I am a historian of China and the broader East Eurasian world roughly from 600 to 1400. For my research, I am working on the intersection of the Chinese, Tibetan, and Iranian worlds and how people traveled through it. The largest corpus of materials that I deal with is written in Chinese, and at IQSS I will be working on the digital analysis of Chinese texts and lives.

item.title: Wei Luo
item.description:
Wei Luo

Associate, Center for Geographic Analysis
Research Fellow in Pediatrics, Harvard Medical School
email wei.luo@childrens.harvard.edu

Dr. Wei Luo is a Research Associate at the Computational Health Informatics Program (CHIP) at Boston Children's Hospital and Harvard Medical School, and an Associate at the Center for Geographic Analysis at Harvard University. He has broad training backgrounds in Geography (GIScience), Public Health (Epidemiology), Computer Science (Visual Analytics), and Network Science. He proposed a new research area: geo-social visual analytics, which aims to integrate spatial analysis, social network analysis, and machine learning into visual analytics environments. He has designed and developed a series of geovisual analytics tools and high performance computational models for infectious disease transmission and control (i.e., influenza, HIV), international trade, social media, crime analysis, climate change impacts, and water crisis.

item.title: Wei
item.description:
Wei-Fang Hsieh

PhD Student in Linguistics, GSAS
email weifanghsieh@g.harvard.edu

Wei-Fang is a third year PhD student in the Department of Linguistics at Harvard. Her main research areas are syntax, semantics, the syntax-semantics interface and laboratory phonology. Her research interests include the syntax and semantics of reduplication, ellipsis and resultatives, and the phonetics and phonology of tone sandhi. Wei-Fang uses experimental methodology to investigate the tone sandhi pattern in Mandarin-English code-switching context. She seeks to relate the empirical data collected using experimental methods to the phonological theory. In particular, her current phonology project investigates how the phonotactic differences between Mandarin and English may have a distance effect on the application of tone sandhi in speech production of Mandarin native speakers code-switching between the two languages. Before coming to Harvard, Wei-Fang received her M.A. in Linguistics and B.A. in Foreign Languages and Literatures from National Chiao Tung University, Taiwan.

item.title: Wanchun Nie
item.description:
Wanchun Nie

Fellow, China Biographical Database Project (CBDB)
email wanchunnie@fas.harvard.edu

Wanchun’s research focuses on political institutions from Han to Tang Dynasty. In addition, in the CBDB project, she is concerned with how digital humanities tools can be used to analyze families and regional groups in fence towns (fanzhen).

item.title: Vinay Mehra
item.description:
Vinay Mehra

Associate of IQSS
email vmehra@iq.harvard.edu

Vinay Mehra is passionate about curating academic literature to make new knowledge accessible to a broader audience, highlighting the crucial role of social science and data in shaping our understanding of the world. His research focuses on identifying and nurturing social science ideas that can be developed into commercial ventures for public benefit. With a strong background in the media and information sectors, Vinay has held leadership roles at Politico and The Boston Globe and has successfully launched multiple companies as an entrepreneur in the data and information space.

item.title: Uma M Ilavarasan
item.description:
Uma M Ilavarasan

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email uilavarasan@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Uma Ilavarasan is a graduate student in the Department of Government at Harvard University, where she studies American and comparative politics, with a focus on American political development, comparative political economy, bureaucratic politics, and social and industrial policy. She employs both qualitative (interview and archival work) and quantitative methods in her work.

item.title: Tucker Sigourney
item.description:
Tucker Sigourney

Postdoctoral Fellow, Human Flourishing Program
email tsigourney@comcast.net
laptop_windows Website

Tucker is a John and Daria Barry postdoctoral fellow at the Human Flourishing Program. His main areas of study are ethics and action theory, especially within the broadly Aristotelian tradition, and his work so far has focused primarily on questions of forgiveness and love. He holds a Ph.D. in philosophy from Florida State University and undergraduate degrees in philosophy and physics from Grove City College.

item.title: Toshiaki Komura
item.description:
Toshiaki Komura

PhD Student in Social and Behavioral Sciences, Chan School of Public Health
email tkomura@g.harvard.edu

Toshiaki Komura is a PhD student in Social and Behavioral Sciences at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. His research interests are family dynamics and health, causal inference methods, and machine learning approaches. Before joining the program, he completed a Master’s in epidemiology and biostatistics at Boston University School of Public Health.

item.title: Thierry Warin
item.description:
Thierry Warin

Visiting Scholar, Digital Data Design (D^3) Institute
email thierry.warin@mail.analytics.hbs.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LinkedIn

A Visiting Scholar at the Digital Data Design (D^3) Institute, Thierry Warin is an academic whose professional pursuits are deeply rooted in the application of data science methodologies to explore global transformations, including phenomena such as the platform economy and the dynamics of innovation. His research is characterized by the examination of institutions, economic dynamics, and the utilization of unstructured data to uncover insights into these complex areas. 
Warin places a high value on democratizing access to the burgeoning field of data science, viewing it as a pivotal step toward collaboratively addressing global challenges. His approach to research is fundamentally inductive, prioritizing a bottom-up methodology that is deeply rooted in data. This perspective not only enriches empirical understanding across various domains but also serves as a robust framework for testing, refining, and potentially redefining theoretical constructs and traditional economic indicators.

PhD Candidate, Statistics

Théo is a first year Ph.D. student at the Harvard Statistics Department. Trained both as a mathematician and social scientist, he specializes in computational methods for ecological inference, sequence and text analysis, and causal inference. He has studied the evolution of revolving doors patterns among French elite civil servants since the 1990s, as well as the causal relationship between company mergers and employment.

item.title: Sung In Kim
item.description:
Sung In Kim

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email sunginkim@g.harvard.edu

Sung In Kim is a PhD student in the Department of Government at Harvard University. She studies comparative political economy, international political economy, and political behavior. Her research focuses on how structural changes in economy affect political attitudes toward immigration and redistribution as well as voting behavior.

item.title: Sun Young Park
item.description:
Sun Young Park

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email sunyoungpark@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Sun Young Park is a PhD candidate in the Department of Government at Harvard University. She studies political behavior and electoral politics in advanced democracies. Her research addresses how political divisions and animosities between social groups are shaped by the interplay of economic structure, social environments, and psychological mechanisms. To theorize the interactions of these macro-, meso-, and micro-level factors, she combines perspectives of political economy, political sociology, and political psychology. Specifically, her work has focused on why gender and geographic divides in political attitudes arise in contemporary democracies. She uses big data analysis, deep learning, spatial analysis, statistical causal inference, and survey methodology to answer these questions.

Science and Technology Studies Fellow, Harvard Kennedy School

laptop_windows Website

Shira Zilberstein is a PhD candidate in sociology at Harvard University and a fellow in the Science and Technology Studies program. Her focuses on cultural sociology, science and technology studies and organizations, as well as theory and methods. She is interested in the production, interpretation and evaluation of ideas and the dynamics between hegemonic and counter-hegemonic forms of knowledge in institutional and technical settings. She focuses on qualitative and computational methods. Her dissertation focuses on applied interdisciplinary research collaborations and the ways in which knowledge is put into practice to define and address social problems.

item.title: Shannon Lynn Parker
item.description:
Shannon Lynn Parker

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email shannonparker@g.harvard.edu

Shannon is a Ph.D. candidate in the Department of Government. Her research focuses on violence against women (particularly women of color), state government, local civil courts, and political attitudes and behaviors. Her dissertation grounds these issues in the United States justice system, while smaller research projects focus on how privacy is defined in this and other contexts in China. Prior to arriving at Harvard, she was a Research Associate in the Liberty & National Security Program at the Brennan Center for Justice at NYU School of Law. She also lived in Beijing as a Princeton in Asia fellow and conducted research in Qingdao as a fellow in the Fulbright-Hays Group Program Abroad. She graduated from Brown University with an A.B. in Public Policy & American Institutions and East Asian Studies.

item.title: Seth Soderborg
item.description:
Seth Soderborg

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email soderborg@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

I am a PhD student in government with research in the world's third- and fourth-largest democracies—Indonesia and Brazil. The goal of my research is to understand how developing country governments conduct public administration under hard research constraints, identify consistent biases in the procedures used to collect government data in these countries, and, separately, to build comprehensive databases of historical election results. With Daniel Ziblatt, I have worked on using local election data from 1930s Germany to study party politics. With Horacio Larreguy and Stephen Ansolabehere advising, I have run my own work on collecting precinct-level election data from printed archives, carrying out election forensics analysis of those results to study election fraud, and making data available in usable forms for researchers and the public in Brazil and Indonesia.

item.title: Seth Rosenberg
item.description:
Seth Rosenberg

Undergraduate Student, Harvard College
email sethrosenberg@college.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Seth Rosenberg studies power. Specifically, He works to conceptually clarify and measure power in international relations (IR). What is power, and how can we talk about it? Who holds power, and how does it manifest in individuals, groups, nations, and other actors? Which methods of exercising power are most effective? These questions have endlessly fascinated Rosenberg as an instrument and researcher of IR.

Currently enrolled in the MMSc Global Health Delivery program at Harvard Medical School, he holds the prestigious Fulbright and Fayat Scholarships. Driven by a passion for global and social health, his work primarily centers on (mental) healthcare and a keen focus on the availability and accessibility of (mental) healthcare services.

Sarah Mohamed in a PhD Candidate in Government. She studies comparative politics and international relations with a focus on the Middle East and North Africa. Specifically, she is interested in political communication and public opinion. Methodologically, her research employs text analysis and survey experiments. Prior to coming to Harvard, Sarah worked in Washington D.C. at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, earned her MPhil in Modern Middle Eastern Studies at St. Antony’s College, Oxford, and her BA in Political Science and Comparative Literature at the University of California, Berkeley.

item.description:
Sarah Farnsworth Hatch

PhD Student in Education, Graduate School of Education
email shatch@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Sarah Farnsworth Hatch is a doctoral student studying prevention science and implementation science at the Harvard Graduate School of Education. She focuses on evaluating programs and practices which support children's social and emotional development, such as parenting interventions and school-based social and emotional learning (SEL). She also studies implementation barriers to these programs, and her current work is centered around politicized opposition to SEL and parents' censorship of schooling materials. She holds an MSEd in Teaching, Learning, and Leadership from the University of Pennsylvania and a BS in Sociology from Brigham Young University.

item.title: Sara Ji
item.description:
Sara Ji

PhD Student in Education Policy, GSAS
email saraji@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Sara is a first year PhD student and a presidential scholar at Harvard studying education policy and the economics of education. Sara’s main research interests lie in applied econometrics, the economics of education, and labor economics, with a focus on inequality. She is passionate about using quantitative methods to understand and improve inequality in education and the labor market. Prior to graduate studies, Sara worked as a Pre-Doctoral research fellow at the National Bureau of Economic Research on projects related to the impact of a desegregation busing program on student outcomes. She also worked as a Research Associate at Harvard Business School. Sara graduated from Brandeis University in 2021 with a BS in applied math, a BA in economics and a minor in philosophy.

Graduate Student, Department of Economics, GSAS

Through IQSS, I have had the opportunity to quantitatively analyze the plight of German workers in response to their local business tax, as well as the determinants and tax-induced effects of on foreign investment.

item.description:
Sakurako S Okuzono

PhD Candidate in Population Health Sciences, Department of Social Behavioral Sciences, Chan School of Public Health
email sshiba@hsph.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Google

Sakurako is a PhD candidate in the Population Health Sciences Program at the Harvard TH Chan School of Public Health. Her research focuses on the following areas: 1) understanding the social determinants of children's health and well-being, 2) examining the impact of natural disasters, and 3) applying computational methods, including machine learning algorithms, to the measurement of social phenomena. This includes exploring the complex, interconnected nature of composite indices, using remote sensing data to measure neighborhood trends over time, and employing a stochastic intervention framework.

item.title: Ruofan Ma
item.description:
Ruofan Ma

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email ruofan_ma@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
  • Twitter

Ruofan Ma is a PhD student in the Government Department at Harvard University. His research interests include international institutions, international trade, labor rights, and human rights. In particular, his works focus on the effects of international organizations and multinational firms on domestic labor standards. Before starting his PhD, Ruofan completed a BA in Politics at Peking University and an AM in East Asia Studies at Harvard.

item.title: Rui Zhao
item.description:
Rui Zhao

SEAS, IACS
email ruz466@g.harvard.edu
My research interest mainly lies in the area of biostatistics and Data science. I used to carry out a research at harvard T.H. Chan school of public health, about applying consensus-based algorithm to high-dimensional geNome data. Currently I am working at IQSS on manuscript peer review and measurement of Novelty.

item.title: Ruby Zhang
item.description:
Ruby Zhang

PhD Student, Department of Economics, GSAS
email rzhang15@g.harvard.edu

Ruby studies innovation and the economics of science using tools from industrial organization. Currently, she is studying the role of scientific capital on research productivity and the role of FCC regulations in shaping the consumer electronics market. She holds a BA degree in Economics from the University of Chicago.

item.title: Roza Nalbandyan
item.description:
Roza Nalbandyan

Postdoctoral Fellow, Human Flourishing Program
email rozanalbandyan@fas.harvard.edu

Roza Nalbandyan is a Postdoctoral Fellow with the Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University. Her research develops and applies advanced statistical and measurement models — including psychometrics, machine learning, and computational methods — to examine measurement comparability, validity, student development, and academic flourishing. She received her Ph.D. in Education (Measurement, Assessment, and Evaluation) and M.S. in Statistics from Stanford University, and an Ed.M. in Higher and Postsecondary Education from Columbia University, Teachers College. Her work has been recognized with multiple fellowships and awards, including the 2026 NCBE Validity & Fairness Research Award, the James and Viola Quillen Fellowship, and the AERA Outstanding Research Award. Before pursuing her doctorate, she spent over six years in university administration, an experience that continues to ground her research in the practical realities of education.

item.title: Roy Guyton
item.description:
Roy Guyton

IT Client Support Services Associate
email rguyton@hmdc.harvard.edu

Roy is a graduate of Willamette University where he received a BA in Spanish Literature and International Studies in Salem, OR. He also received his Ed.M for Technology in Education from the Harvard Graduate School of Education. Additionally, Roy has taken graduate courses at NIH, MIT and the Division of Continuing Education (DCE) at Harvard University. Roy was employed with the Harvard Graduate School of Education for 12 years where he ran the student help desk, and employed 10 years at DCE as a graduate teaching assistant. He spent three years in Costa Rica as a US Peace Corps volunteer working with IBM , MIT, Omar Dengo Foundation and the Costa Rican Department of Education on a national computer education program. He holds extensive contracting experience since 1992 in the area of technology in education. Roy was also a board member of several non profit organizations.

item.title: Rony Rodrigo Maximiliano Rodriguez
item.description:
Rony Rodrigo Maximiliano Rodriguez-Ramirez

PhD Candidate in Education Policy and Program Evaluation, HGSE
email rrodriguezramirez@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows GitHub

Rony Rodriguez is a PhD student in Education Policy and Program Evaluation at Harvard University whose work centers on understanding and mitigating the impacts of major disruptions, such as pandemics, conflict, and displacement, on educational outcomes and human capital development. His research investigates how targeted educational and mental health interventions can strengthen student well-being and resilience, reduce inequality, and improve long-term educational and economic trajectories for vulnerable populations. He focuses in particular on school-based interventions like individualized tutoring, enhanced curricula, and integrated mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS). He holds an M.D.P. in Development Policy from KDI School (South Korea) and a B.A. in Applied Economics from Universidad Centroamericana (Nicaragua).

item.title: Ronak Jain
item.description:
Ronak Jain

PhD Candidate, Department of Economics
email rjain@g.harvard.edu

Ronak Jain is a PhD Candidate in Economics at Harvard University. Her research interests include development and behavioral economics. She did her MPhil in Economics at the University of Oxford and undergraduate from the University of Cambridge.

item.title: Roman Klimke
item.description:
Roman Klimke

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, HKS and GSAS
email klimke@fas.harvard.edu

Roman Klimke is a Ph.D. candidate in Public Policy at Harvard University. His research interests lie at the intersection of labor and public economics. His current research investigates how labor markets respond to the introduction of social programs, for which he uses individual- and establishment-level Social Security data from Germany and Saudi Arabia. Roman holds a B.Sc. degree in International Economics and Middle Eastern Studies from the University of Tuebingen and spent one year abroad at the University of Michigan during his undergraduate studies.

item.title: Rochelle Sun
item.description:
Rochelle Sun

PhD Student in Government, GSAS
laptop_windows Website

Rochelle Sun is a PhD student in the Department of Government studying the role of healthcare in diplomacy and security. Her research seeks to understand how states and non-state actors strategically use mechanisms of healthcare to achieve political objectives, and under what circumstances these strategies are successful. She also supports research optimizing infectious disease care in conflict settings through her collaboration with GHESKIO, a healthcare and research center in Port-au-Prince, Haiti. She is a member of the associate board for the Alliance for International Medical Action (ALIMA).

item.title: Robert Manduca
item.description:
Robert Manduca

Sociology and Social Policy, GSAS
email rmanduca@g.harvard.edu
Robert Manduca is a PhD student in Sociology & Social Policy. Robert is interested in the ecoNomic outcomes of people and places: why some cities and regions do well while others do poorly, and how individuals are and are Not able to participate in the ecoNomic growth of their regions. He studies these topics primarily through the use of quantitative methods on large administrative datasets gathered from governments, corporations, and the Internet. Before starting graduate school, he worked as a consultant in fair lending regulation, testing for discrimination in mortgage lending.

item.title: Robert Gaudin, MD
item.description:
Robert Gaudin, MD

Oral-Maxillofacial and Plastic Surgery, Charité, Berlin
Visiting Fellow
email robert-andre.gaudin@charite.de

Doctor Gaudin graduated from Medical School, Hamburg, Germany and defended his doctoral thesis in 2014. From January 2015 until June 2016 he was a research fellow at Harvard Medical School, Department of Facial Plastics MGH/MEEI working on the development of a Bionic Face in an animal model. Besides that he was working on  basic clinical studies. In July 2016 he started working as a resident at the University Clinic, Hamburg, Eppendorf, Germany at the Department Oral- Maxillofacial and Plastic Surgery. Besides his residency he has started studying dentistry at the University of Kiel in October 2016. Since then, it was of great interest for doctor Gaudin to find out how well doctors do on rating dental x-rays, since it is the number one diagnostic tool in the clinic. Also, if it is possible to create a superhuman algorithm diagnosing pathologies to improve the doctor's rating process. This is how he got involved with the Lab of Karim Lakhani at Harvard. Besides that he is doing Research on 3D printing of tissue. Since February 2017 doctor Gaudin switched his residency position to the Department Oral- Maxillofacial and Plastic Surgery, Charite, Berlin and continued his projects from there.

item.title: Reed Rasband
item.description:
Reed Rasband

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email rasband@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Reed Rasband is a PhD Candidate at Harvard's Department of Government, studying comparative politics. He studies identity politics, public opinion and intergroup relations in the Global South, with an emphasis on migration within Latin America. His work incorporates a variety of methodological approaches, including survey data, interviews, and experiments. His dissertation investigates how Venezuelan migration has influenced relations between citizens and migrants in Chile and Brazil. He has also carried out research in Colombia, Thailand, and the United Kingdom.

PhD Candidate, Economics

Raphaël is specialized in behavioral and political economics, with a special focus on experimental methods. His research tries to combine insights from psychology with quantitative methods of economic science in order to answer questions of social or political relevance. His recent projects study the role of audience effects on the public display of social preferences, and the psychology of human-AI interactions.

item.title: Queenie Luo
item.description:
Queenie Luo

Data Scientist, IQSS Fellow, China Biographical Database Project
Ph.D. Candidate, Department of East Asian Languages and Civilizations
email queenieluo@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Queenie Luo graduated summa cum laude from Columbia University with a B.A. in Religion and Physics, and completed her M.T.S in Buddhist Studies at Harvard Divinity School. Before coming to Columbia, Queenie immersed herself in the Fashion Industry. At age nineteen, she became the youngest designer to have couture collections presented at Vancouver Fashion Week and went on to spend three years studying fashion design at Parsons School of Design in New York. In 2018, Queenie received the Phi Beta Kappa Prize, which is awarded to the candidate who best represents the ideals of the society—intellectual integrity, tolerance for other views, and a broad range of academic interests. Her current research is situated at the intersection of religion and science.

item.title: Qi Liu
item.description:
Qi Liu

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email qi_liu@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Personal website

Qi Liu is a PhD student in the department of Government at Harvard University. Her research interests are in international relations and political methodology, with a focus on international political economy and international institutions. She is especially interested in how states cooperate within the international regime complex. Prior to coming to Harvard, Qi earned a B.A. in Economics and International Relations from Peking University and an M.A. in Political Science from Duke University.

item.title: Priyanka Sethy
item.description:
Priyanka Sethy

PhD Candidate, Department of Government, GSAS
email psethy@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Priyanka is a PhD student in Comparative Politics. She focuses on social identity politics, race and ethnicity in politics, and immigrants and immigration. Her current projects include studying how diaspora communities engage with the politics of their origin countries, how natives form attitudes towards immigrants, and how immigrants form preferences around immigration.

item.title: Priya Shanmugam
item.description:
Priya Shanmugam

Department of Economics, GSAS
email priyavshanmugam@fas.harvard.edu
Priya is a second-year Ph.D. student in the Department of Economics. She is broadly interested in labor ecoNomics and the ecoNomics of social networks. Current projects include the heuristic basis of decision fatigue using data from the New Hampshire prison parole system, the role of local natural resource booms and busts on educational attainment behavior, and the mechanism of peer effects in medical treatment settings. Priya earned her B.A. in Economics from Dartmouth College in 2013.

item.title: Prachi Sanghavi
item.description:
Prachi Sanghavi

Graduate Student, Program in Health Policy
location_on CGIS Knafel K311
smartphone 617-496-3798
email sanghav@fas.harvard.edu
Prachi Sanghavi is a 3rd year PhD student in the Program in Health Policy at the Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, and in the Evaluative Science and Statistics concentration of the program. She is interested in a range of subjects around violence and injuries, socio-ecoNomic determinants of health, and the roles of corporations in healthcare decisions. Her dissertation will explore the cost-effectiveness of different techNologies used in pre-hospital care. Prior to coming to Harvard, Prachi researched fire-related deaths among women in India, which are often related with domestic violence. Prachi graduated from Cornell University with a B.S. in Electrical and Computer Engineering, and has spent a few years as a management consultant in New York City. She is the recipient of a National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship.

item.title: Philip Johnston
item.description:
Philip Johnston

Harvard GSAS, Department of Near Eastern Languages and Civilizations
email philip.a.johnston@gmail.com
I am an archaeologist interested in the origins and life cycles of ecoNomic and social institutions. My work is primarily in Iron Age Southwestern Spain (c. 825-550 BC), and in the Middle Bronze-Iron Ages in the Southern Levant (c. 1925-586 BC). My dissertation examines the social repercussions of regional ecoNomic specialization at the site of El Castillo de Doña Blanca (Spain), using pottery analysis to shed light on the organization of production, modes of exchange, and techNological inNovation/dissemination among Phoenician and indigeNous potters.

item.description:
Olivia Woldemikael

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email woldemikael@g.harvard.edu

Olivia Woldenmikael's research focuses on the attitudes of host communities towards refugees and immigrants in the Global South. Her dissertation project aims to identify the determinants of individual-level attitudes, explaining why certain individuals and communities are receptive to forced migrants and others are hostile.

Olivia So

IQSS Computer Lab Assistant
Graduate Student, GSD

Olivia So is pursuing her Master in Landscape Architecture I at Harvard Graduate School of Design. Olivia completed an undergraduate degree in Urban and Regional Studies. She hopes to continue exploring the intersection of urban planning and landscape architecture through both policy and physical design. As a previous user of the IQSS Lab, she wants to help support others in the lab.

PhD Candidate in Sociology, GSAS

Ohjae Gowen is a doctoral student in Sociology at Harvard University. He studies causes and consequences of childbearing, with a particular focus on gendered labor market dynamics. He is interested in applying quantitative methods to analyzing longitudinal data. Before coming to Harvard, Ohjae received his BA and MA in Sociology from Seoul National University, South Korea. He is the recipient of the scholarship funded by the Korea Foundation for Advanced Studies.

PhD Student in Public Policy, GSAS

Nikhil is a graduate student in the PhD in Public Policy program (Economics Track). He is interested in research on economic development and political economy, and he focuses on how education, migration, state capacity and infrastructure shape socio-economic outcomes in India. Prior to his PhD, he worked as a research fellow at Harvard Kennedy School. He completed his undergraduate studies in Materials Science and Engineering from Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (Mumbai, India) and an MA in Economic Policy from Boston University.

item.description:

IQSS Graduate Student Affiliate

Nicole Letourneau is a doctoral student in the Department of Sociology at Harvard University. Nicole employs a multi-method approach to explore questions related to the influence of climate change on community priorities and investments among various stakeholders in urban settings. Originally from North Carolina, she completed her bachelor’s degree in history at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. She received graduate degrees in public health from Emory University and in anthropology from the University of California, San Diego.

item.title: Nicolas Wicaksono
item.description:
Nicolas Wicaksono

PhD Student, Department of Government, GSAS
email nwicaksono@g.harvard.edu

Nic is broadly interested in the political economy of development and humanitarian assistance, particularly in conflict-affected settings. His specific interests include governance by non-state actors, the short- and long-term legacies of political violence, identity politics, and research methods for measuring sensitive attitudes and behaviors. He was previously based in Somaliland and worked for Consilient, where he managed a portfolio of program evaluation and formative research projects on behalf of development and humanitarian partners, including UN agencies, USAID, the UK’s FCDO, CARE International, and Mercy Corps. He also worked for Search for Common Ground, where he researched the effectiveness of social cohesion programs and helped implement an initiative in Indonesia to facilitate the social reintegration of individuals formerly incarcerated on terrorism charges. Nic holds a BA in Sociology and Global Affairs from Yale University and a master’s in Global Affairs from Schwarzman College, Tsinghua University.

PhD Student in Sociology and Social Policy, GSAS

Nicholas (Nicc) Forster-Benson is a doctoral student in Sociology and Social Policy at Harvard University. Broadly, his research investigates how various market structures and interventions affect economic inequalities at both the local and global scale. Currently, Nicc’s research focuses on US housing markets, examining the intersection of local politics, financialization, liquidity, and inequality.

item.description:
Molly Hickey

PhD Candidate, Department of Government, GSAS
email mollyhickey@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Molly Hickey is a PhD candidate in the Harvard Government Department. She studies the political economy of authoritarianism in the Middle East with an emphasis on labor policy, welfare, and foreign aid. Prior to beginning her PhD at Harvard, she worked on USAID projects in the MENA/Afghanistan region and was a 2019-2020 Fulbright Student Fellow in Jordan, where she researched the politics of Syrian refugee labor permits and closed professions.

item.title: Mitsuru Mukaigawara
item.description:
Mitsuru Mukaigawara

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email mitsuru_mukaigawara@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Mitsuru Mukaigawara is a PhD student in Government at Harvard University. Prior to graduate school, he worked globally both as an infectious disease doctor and a policy analyst, from a remote island in southern Japan to the World Health Organization headquarters. He studies international relations, with a focus on the interaction between disease epidemics and international security. He received an MD from Tokyo Medical and Dental University and an MPP from Harvard Kennedy School, where he was an inaugural Graham T. Allison, Jr. Student Fellow and a recipient of the Robert Belfer Annual Award for the best master's thesis in international relations.

item.title: Misha Teplitskiy
item.description:
Misha Teplitskiy

Postdoctoral Fellow
Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard
email mteplitskiy@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows LISH
Misha Teplitskiy is a Postdoctoral Fellow at the Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard (LISH). Previously, he was in the Sociology PhD program at the University of Chicago, where he worked with James Evans to apply new computational social science techniques to study the evaluation, robustness, and diffusion of scientific kNowledge. His research bridges Sociology of Science and Management - he studies how institutional and organizational factors affect scientific discovery and other domains of collaborative kNowledge production. Two core themes are: Resource allocation: How are financial and reputational resources allocated within the scientific community, and can these allocations be improved? He works with funding agencies and scientific journals to identify how the selection processes they use, such as peer review and expert committees, predispose them (often unintentionally) to select particular winners or losers, and how these decisions impact the type of science that gets done. Influence and valuation: Can the value of a scientific work be measured? He uses computational surveys to explore what types of works scientists value, how they influence one aNother, and whether these networks of influence can be distilled from the scientific literature. He aims to develop methods with which we can more effectively identify and allocate resources to important research. Methodologically, he specializes in computational analysis of large datasets, field experiments, and targeted surveys. He applies these tools to data and experimental settings provided by partners like Harvard Medical School, Conquer Cancer, Elsevier, and Public Library of Science.

item.title: Milo Trujillo
item.description:
Milo Trujillo

PhD student, Complex Systems and Data Science, University of Vermont
email milo.trujillo@uvm.edu
laptop_windows Website
Milo's research applies network science and complex systems to understanding intentional community design. His past work includes propaganda simulations, constraint-based automatic community design, analysis of alt-tech social platforms, and coordinating volunteer groups through the Pursuance Project. Milo is working with IQSS to recreate network structure and community behavior patterns from limited organizational data.

item.title: Mikko Silliman
item.description:
Mikko Silliman

Graduate Student, Education Policy & Program Evaluation, GSAS/HGSE
email silliman@g.harvard.edu
Mikko Silliman is a Ph.D. student in Education Policy & Program Evaluation interested in issues at the intersection of education policy and labor economics - particularly as they relate to inequality. His research focuses on applying quasi-experimental designs to learn from administrative data sets. Before beginning his doctoral studies, he conducted research at the VATT Institute for Economic Research and the OECD. He holds a MSSc in Social & Public Policy from the University of Helsinki, a BA in Economics and in Philosophy, summa cum laude, from Tufts University, and was a Visiting Student at the University of Oxford.

item.title: Michael Zhao
item.description:
Michael Zhao

Concentrator in Statistics & Computer Science, Harvard College
email michaelzhao@college.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

 

Michael Zhao is a junior from California living in Quincy House and concentrating in Statistics and Computer Science with a secondary in Government. His principal research interests lie in the development and novel cross-application of computational techniques to areas in government and law.

item.title: Michael Zanger
item.description:
Michael Zanger-Tishler

PhD Candidate in Sociology and Social Policy, GSAS
email michael_zangertishler@g.harvard.edu

Michael Zanger-Tishler is a PhD student in Sociology & Social Policy. He is interested in understanding the relationship between race, ethnicity, and the criminal justice system in a comparative context, specifically looking at the United States, Israel, and Europe. Michael is interested in exploring quantitative tools that can be used to explore the comparative criminalization of minorities.

item.title: Michael Walsh McGarrah
item.description:
Michael Walsh McGarrah

Visiting Scholar, HGSE
email michaelmcgarrah@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Michael's research identifies risk factors for early substance use among children and parses the dimensionality of psychopathology, more broadly, in terms of emergent internalizing and externalizing behaviors. He uses advanced psychometric and applied statistical methods in the field of structural equation modelling to explore these topics, including latent class growth models and auto-regressive latent trajectory models with structured residuals. During the 2023-2024 school year, Michael will publish results from his dissertation on early substance use among elementary school children in order to inform substance use prevention and intervention efforts aimed at reducing the harm of legal and illicit substances on youth and the incidence and prevalence of substance use disorders, such as alcoholism and opioid addiction.

A secondary research interest that Michal explores using more traditional methods explores the psychology of curiosity, including inter- and intra-individual differences in information-seeking behavior and possible targets for intervention to cultivate a strong intellectual appetite an intellectually promiscuous mindset.

item.title: Michael Olson
item.description:
Michael Olson

Government, GSAS
email michaelolson@g.harvard.edu
Michael is a first-year graduate student in the Department of Government studying American Politics, with particular interests in legislative institutions, legislator behavior, elections, and U.S. state politics. He seeks to leverage the comparative scope of the fifty states to gain insight into the impact of institutional design on behavioral, policy, and electoral outcomes. Before coming to Harvard, Michael earned his B.A. in Political Science from Vanderbilt University, as well as serving as a Legislative Intern in the Tennessee House of Representatives.

Graduate Student, Department of Government, GSAS

She is currently working on projects using text analysis to measure racial attitudes and exploring survey non-response bias using voter files. Prior to arriving at Harvard she worked in political analytics, doing polling and data science for progressive organizations and campaigns.

item.title: Max Spohn
item.description:
Max Spohn

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, HKS
laptop_windows Twitter

 

Max is a PhD candidate in Public Policy at the Harvard Kennedy School. He is interested in economic and political beliefs, such as election denialism, perceptions of social mobility, or views on high-risk investments. He combines methods and insights from Psychology, Economics, and Political Science to better understand how beliefs form, how they impact people's decisions, and the role of narratives in sustaining or changing them.

item.title: Matt Reichert
item.description:
Matt Reichert

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email mreichert@g.harvard.edu

I am a PhD Candidate in the Department of Government. I am interested in the politics of state formation and nation formation, especially in post-revolutionary settings. I am also interested in democratization, democratic breakdown, and civil conflict. My current work focuses on state-building in the Soviet Union under high Stalinism, and examines the consequences of ethnically-based hiring and mass political purges for bureaucratic quality. Before coming to Harvard, I worked on model-based forecasting for the defense contracting community.

item.title: Mathew Kiang
item.description:
Mathew Kiang

Harvard School of Public Health, Department of Social and Behavioral Sciences
email mkiang@mail.harvard.edu
Mathew Kiang is a first-year doctoral student at the Harvard School of Public Health studying social epidemiology. His research interests include the application of network analysis and agent-based modeling to understanding health disparities and health behaviors especially with regard to HIV. Before starting the doctoral program, Matt was a research assistant at HSPH in Health Policy and Management. He holds an MPH from New York University, where his independent research and master's thesis focused on HIV inequalities in townships near Cape Town, South Africa, and a bachelor's degree in Sociology and Social Inequalities from San Diego State University.

item.title: Marysol Fernández Harvey
item.description:
Marysol Fernández Harvey

PhD Student in Government and Social Policy, GSAS
email mfernandezharvey@fas.harvard.edu

Marysol is a PhD student of American and Comparative Politics in the Government and Social Policy Program. She seeks to bridge siloes between US domestic and international scholarship on democratic strengthening. She is interested in literatures on conflict transformation, civil resistance, peacebuilding, and American social movement studies, in exploring an expanded tactical toolbox for combating democratic backsliding and increasing inequality in the United States. She employs a mixed methods approach to the evaluation of the comparative effectiveness across a diversity of tactics and approaches available to civil society actors in protecting and expanding democratic practice.

Prior to Harvard, Marysol worked as a Research and Organizing Fellow at the Horizons Project supporting peacebuilders, activists, and other practitioners in the movement for a just, inclusive democracy. Additionally, she worked as a social impact consultant where I partnered with philanthropic leaders to build funding and capacity building infrastructure in support of movements for lasting systems change.

Marysol received her BA in Economics and Comparative Literature at Brown University and was awarded the national Truman Scholarship for leadership, public service, and academic achievement.

item.title: Mary Laski
item.description:
Mary Laski

PhD Candidate, Graduate School of Education
email marylaski@g.harvard.edu

Mary Laski is a PhD candidate in Education at Harvard University and a PIER Fellow at the Center for Education Policy Research. She studies education policy and the economics of education, with a particular focus on teacher quality, teacher labor markets, and the principal's role in school improvement.

Before coming to Harvard, Mary managed a research project at Brown University in partnership with the Tennessee Department of Education. She holds both an A.M. in Urban Education Policy and an A.B. in Economics and American Studies from Brown University.

item.title: Martin Koenen
item.description:
Martin Koenen

PhD Candidate in Political Economy and Government, GSAS
email martin_koenen@g.harvard.edu

Martin Koenen is a PhD candidate in Political Economy and Government. His research interests are in political economy, labor economics and public finance. Within these fields, he is primarily interested in the causes and consequences of economic inequality as well as questions of political polarization.

Before beginning his PhD, Martin was a pre-doctoral fellow at Opportunity Insights working on research related to economic opportunity and social mobility. Martin holds a M.Sc. and a B.Sc. In Economics from the University of Mannheim and Humboldt-University, Berlin.

item.title: Marie Montás
item.description:
Marie Chantel Montás

PhD Candidate in Global Health and Population, Chan School of Public Health
email mariemontas@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Personal Website
  • Instagram

Marie Chantel Montás is a PhD candidate in Health Systems, Department of Global Health and Population at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. She works at the intersection of development economics and global health. Her research uses quasi-experimental causal inference methods to evaluate the impact of health policies and programs on maternal and infant health, sexual and reproductive health, and pharmaceutical markets. She is particularly interested in health systems and how patients respond to dimensions of care quality, including provider behavior and service delivery. Marie holds an MPH in Health Management and Policy from the University of Michigan and a BA in Economics from Pontificia Universidad Católica Madre y Maestra in Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic.

item.title: Maria Soledad Hershey
item.description:
Maria Soledad Hershey

Research Associate, Human Flourishing Program
email mhershey@fas.harvard.edu

Maria Soledad Hershey’s research focuses on understanding upstream sociocultural and behavioral determinants of health to advance chronic disease prevention and well-being. Her work bridges epidemiology, lifestyle medicine, and human flourishing. She has studied the potential synergistic interaction between the Mediterranean diet and physical activity, and protective associations of other modifiable lifestyle factors using an overall Mediterranean lifestyle index, on premature mortality and chronic disease. Her previous postdoctoral work at the Human Flourishing Program at Harvard University assessed a virtue-based leadership intervention on context-sensitive decision-making and flourishing. She previously completed a postdoctoral fellowship at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health as part of the Harvard Educational Resource Center Training Grant, where she conducted the primary analysis of a randomized controlled trial on adherence to the Mediterranean diet in a non-Mediterranean working population. She is passionate about translating empirical research into holistic, evidence-based interventions and policy advocacy for preventive health and well-being.

item.title: Margot Moinester
item.description:
Margot Moinester

Sociology, GSAS
email mmoinester@fas.harvard.edu
Margot Moinester is a Ph.D. candidate in Sociology at Harvard University, where she is a Doctoral Fellow in the Multidisciplinary Program in Inequality and Social Policy and a Graduate Student Associate at the Weatherhead Center for International Affairs. After graduating from Brandeis University with a BA in Health: Science, Society and Policy, Margot led a regional assessment of access to healthcare in Northeast Montana as an AmeriCorps VISTA volunteer and conducted research with the Women's Interagency HIV Study in Chicago. Her current research interests include immigration enforcement and policy, health inequalities, and crime and punishment.

Prior to Harvard, Marco completed bachelor's and master's degrees in political science at l'Université de Montréal.

item.title: Manuel Meléndez Sánchez
item.description:
Manuel Meléndez Sánchez

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email mmelend@g.harvard.edu

Manuel is a PhD student in the Harvard Government Department. He studies the comparative political economy of development, with an emphasis on political parties, the politics of violence, and how the two interact. His regional focus is on Latin America. Originally from El Salvador, Manuel has an AB from Harvard (2016) and an MSc from Oxford (2017).

item.title: Maleah Fekete
item.description:
Maleah Fekete

Doctoral Candidate, Department of Sociology
I am broadly interested in emotions, gender, mental health, social networks, cultural sociology, and applying cognitive science to sociology. My last project investigated devoted romance reading women. I was especially interested in what it means to be a romance reader, how this identity has changed over the past 35 years, and the implications of changing romantic fantasies on gender relations. My current project investigates the gender gap in depression. I am analyzing time-space variations to understand the relationship between community-specific gender-role cultures and the gender gap.

item.title: Mahesh Karra
item.description:
Mahesh Karra

Graduate Student, Harvard School of Public Health
Global Health Economics
email mkarra@mail.harvard.edu
Mahesh Karra is a second year doctoral student in global health ecoNomics at the Harvard School of Public Health. His academic and research interests are broadly in development ecoNomics, health ecoNomics, quantitative methods, and applied demography. His most recent research focuses on examining the short- and long-term impacts of family planning on fertility, maternal and child health, and downstream ecoNomic outcomes including labor force participation, income growth, and wealth.Prior to coming to Harvard, Mahesh was a policy analyst at the Population Reference Bureau (PRB) in Washington, DC, contributing to dissemination and research activities and providing technical assistance to initiatives associated with the Population and Poverty (PopPov) research network.Mahesh holds a Joint HoNors B.A. in Economics and in Hispanic Studies from McGill University and an M.Sc. in Economics from the Barcelona Graduate School of Economics.

item.title: Magdalena Larreboure
item.description:
Magdalena Larreboure

PhD Student in Public Policy, HKS
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter

Magdalena’s research focuses on political economy using elaborate empirical tools. She is especially interested in the representation of women and minorities in the economy and the public sphere, and in turn, how the right representation improves livelihoods and welfare. She is also working on various projects related to environmental policy, particularly the feasibility of implementing climate policies around the world amidst the growing impact of natural disasters on vulnerable populations.

item.title: Mafalda Pratas Fernandes
item.description:
Mafalda Pratas Fernandes

PhD Candidate, Department of Government, GSAS
email mpratasf@g.harvard.edu

Mafalda Pratas Fernandes is a graduate student in the Department of Government. Her research agenda is at the intersection of comparative political institutions and representation in modern democracies (primarily Europe and North America). In particular, she is interested in the origins and effects of direct vs. representative democratic institutions, the roles of political parties, and the emergence of winners and losers in the process of representation. Methodologically, she is interested in experimental and non-experimental survey research, as well as techniques for causal inference from observational data.

item.title: Lucy Song
item.description:
Lucy Song

PhD Candidate, Department of Government, GSAS
email lucysong@g.harvard.edu

My dissertation examines how social identity determines international migration decisions and how processes of international migration shape social identities among recent migrant cohorts in turn, with consequences for foreign policy and origin-destination country relations. My job market paper focuses on the causes and consequences of “diasporic nationalism” for international politics, with cross-national observational and experimental data, text analysis, and meta-analysis. While my dissertation centers on the prominent case of China and its diaspora, my broader research agenda adopts a global perspective.

item.title: Lucía Mendoza Mora
item.description:
Lucía Mendoza Mora

PhD Student in Government, GSAS
email luciamendozamora@g.harvard.edu

Lucía Mendoza is a PhD student in the Department of Government. She studies the political economy of democratic accountability, corruption, and political violence in Latin America. She holds BAs in Economics and History, and an MA in Economics from Universidad de los Andes in Bogotá, Colombia. Her prior research experience focused on civil war, crime, and police-community relations.

Communications Coordinator

As Communications Coordinator, she oversees the Institute's online presence, publicity, and external communications, as well as updates to the IQSS affiliate database. She came to IQSS from the Harvard Foundation (part of Harvard College Student Life), and before coming to Harvard she worked at the Center for English Language at Boston University.

He holds an AB in Applied Mathematics with a focus in Economics from Harvard College.

item.title: Lena Shi
item.description:
Lena Shi

PhD Candidate in Education Policy, GSE & GSAS
email yulenashi@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Lena Shi is a doctoral candidate in Education & Policy Evaluation. Her research focuses on identifying contributors to and solutions for higher education inequality, especially as they relate to public finance, school quality, diversity & inclusion, and college admissions. These research interests developed from her previous work as a policy advisor at the White House and researcher at the U.S. Department of Education. Lena holds an MPP and BA in Global Development Studies from the University of Virginia.

item.title: Leah U Stein
item.description:
Leah U Stein

PhD Student, Department of Government, GSAS
email lstein@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter/X
laptop_windows Bluesky

Leah studies American political institutions with a focus on corporate political activity at the state level. She is particularly interested in understanding how the relationship between business and government affects job creation in US states. She also studies US federalism and explores how the federalist structure affects the distribution of resources across states and voters’ representation at the national level. Prior to starting at Harvard, Leah received an MA in Politics from New York University and a BA in Economics and Fundamentals: Issues & Texts from the University of Chicago.

Laura Royden

item.title: Laura Rosendahl Huber
item.description:
Laura Rosendahl Huber

Visiting Fellow, LISH
Senior Research Fellow, Max Planck Institute for Innovation and Competition
email rosendahlhuber@rsm.nl

Laura Rosendahl Huber holds a Ph.D. in Economics from the University of Amsterdam and is currently working as a Senior Research Fellow at the Max Planck Institute for Innovation and Competition in Munich, Germany. Her research integrates entrepreneurship research with experimental methods and focuses on the empirical analysis of entrepreneurship education, entrepreneurial teams, and the evaluation of policies aimed at fostering entrepreneurship and innovation. In particular, she is interested in gaining a better understanding of the gender inequality among both entrepreneurs and inventors (and potential solutions), and how team composition affects team performance in creative processes. A common denominator in her research is the use of field experiments.

item.description:
Kiran Ambika Misra

PhD Student in Government, GSAS
email kiranmisra@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Kiran Misra is a PhD student in the American Politics subfield of Harvard's Department of Government. Her substantive research interests include criminalization and the carceral state, disability, and race and immigration. Prior to starting her PhD, she worked as an investigative journalist and as a consultant for the United Nations after receiving both her Master's and Bachelor's in Public Policy with honors from the University of Chicago.

item.title: Kiara Hernandez
item.description:
Kiara Hernandez

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email khernandez@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
Kiara Hernandez a PhD candidate in Government and a James M. and Cathleen D. Stone PhD Scholar in Inequality and Wealth Concentration at the Harvard Kennedy School. Her research interests are in political psychology and political behavior. Her dissertation centers modern-day low-wage service sector workplaces as a way to understand how individuals make sense of social difference, conceptualize hierarchy, and engage in cooperation and competition, and the downstream effects this has on discriminatory political attitudes and behavior.

item.title: Kevin K Troy
item.description:
Kevin K Troy

PhD student in the Department of Government
email kevintroy@g.harvard.edu

Kevin Troy is a PhD student in theDepartment of Government. His research examines the relationship between information and communication technology and political psychology, especially with applications to international security and subnational conflict. He has a particular interest in experimental methods and is a Lab Manager at the Harvard Digital Lab for the Social Sciences. Kevin received an AB in Political Science from Duke University.

item.title: Kentaro Nakamura

PhD Student in Public Policy, GSAS

laptop_windows Website

Kentaro Nakamura is a PhD Student in Public Policy (Politics and Institution track) at Harvard University. His research interests include international relations (especially political violence, civil wars, and misinformation) and political methodologies (especially causal inference and machine learning). Before coming at Harvard, he received MS in Statistics from the University of Chicago and BA in Political Science from Waseda University in Japan.

item.title: Kelsey Pukelis
item.description:
Kelsey Pukelis

PhD Student in Public Policy, GSAS
email kelseypukelis@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Kelsey Pukelis is a PhD student in Public Policy interested in the economics of the social safety net. Her current research focuses on the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program with projects investigating the impacts of work requirements, the effects of drastic administrative changes made to the program since the COVID-19 pandemic, and the nature of stigma associated with participation.

item.title: Katherine Irajpanah
item.description:
Katherine Irajpanah

PhD Candidate, Department of Government, GSAS
email kirajpanah@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Katherine Irajpanah is a Ph.D. candidate in the Department of Government. She is also a 2024-25 International Security Program Pre-Doctoral Fellow at the Belfer Center and Minerva Peace and Security Fellow at the United States Institute of Peace. Her dissertation examines norms around the use of force and patterns in international conflict. In the project, she introduces a conflict dataset (Global Wars, 1800-2022) with new information on the capabilities and behavior of war participants. Her other research takes a text-as-data approach to understanding how intelligence agencies and the military draw assessments about foreign adversaries.

item.title: Katelyn Long
item.description:
Katelyn Long

John and Daria Barry Post-Doctoral Fellow, Human Flourishing Program
Postdoctoral Research Fellow, Chan School of Public Health
email knlong@fas.harvard.edu
Katelyn Long's current work focuses on the group dynamics of religion on human flourishing and the development of tradition-specific spiritual well-being measures. She completed her doctoral studies at Boston University School of Public Health where her dissertation focused on the role of faith-based and charitable health providers in the context of the broader health system. Her other public health work has been in the areas of chronic disease prevention, adolescent health, mental health, and positive deviance in vulnerable communities. She earned her Master of Science in Public Health from the University of Utah and her undergraduate degree in religion with a minor in music from Vanguard University

He is interested in applying industrial organization models to study health care markets for the purpose of aiding health policy and health systems design, specifically in lower-middle income countries.

item.description:
Kadeem Noray

Public Policy

Kadeem Noray is a PhD student in Public Policy at Harvard University. His research interests include labor ecoNomics, public ecoNomics, and applied ecoNometrics. He is currently working on estimating the causal effect of police brutality on local crime rates in Chicago and analyzing the long term impacts of various early 20th century U.S. public health policies. Before coming to Harvard, Kadeem received a BS in Economics, Mathematics, and Physics from Hillsdale College and an MS in Applied Economics from Montana State University.

Jun holds an M.Phil. in economics from the University of Oxford and a Bachelor degree from Tsinghua University.

item.title: Julie Anne Weaver
item.description:
Julie Anne Weaver

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email julieanneweaver@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

I am a PhD candidate in the Department of Government at Harvard University. I research local-level political accountability in developing countries, particularly in Latin America.  My dissertation and book project study what happens when voters try to hold their elected officials accountable in contexts where institutions and political parties are weak.  I use surveys, text analysis, and experimental, qualitative and quantitative data.  I hold a B.A. in Sociology from Barnard College and an M.A. in International Development from the Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies (SAIS).

item.title: José Ramón Enríquez
item.description:
José Ramón Enríquez

Ph.D. Candidate in Political Economy and Government
email jrenriquez@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

José Ramón Enríquez is a third-year Ph.D. student in Political Economy and Government. His interests lie in the political economy of development and comparative economic development with a particular interest in elite behavior. Specifically, he has worked on the causes and effects of democratic political dynasties, the role of information in improving political accountability, and the link between crime/violence and public good provision/democratic representation. In his current projects, he uses data from Mexico.

Before beginning his studies at Harvard, he obtained a double bachelor in Economics and Political Science from Instituto Tecnológico Autónomo de México (ITAM) in Mexico City.

item.title: Jonathan Phillips
item.description:
Jonathan Phillips

Government Department, GSAS

My research focuses on how relationships between politicians and citizens can change, and specifically on when effective accountability relationships can be rapidly created. When do exploited clients--dependent on political connections for access to public services--become empowered citizens--able to claim objective rights to public services? In Brazil, India and Nigeria, using localized measures of public services I use spatial analysis to identify how experiences with the state can alter the demands voters place on government: the claims they make and the implicit voting rules they apply at the ballot box. In Ghana, under an IQSS project, I have supported the Center for Democracy and Development to generate and disseminate information on public services in order to investigate how information builds public accountability.

item.title: Jonathan Ji
item.description:
Jonathan Ji

IQSS Computer Lab Assistant
email jonathanji@hds.harvard.edu

Jonathan is a Master of Theological Studies candidate at Harvard Divinity School. He is interested in American religious history, specifically the Metropolitan Community Churches during the AIDS epidemic. He graduated from New York University with a BA in Individualized Study.

He is a Visiting Scholar at the Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard, Institute for Quantitative Social Sciences (IQSS).Jonas' research focuses on incentives, behavior and performance of individuals in tournaments. In particular he investigates the effect of pressure due to heterogeneous abilities of contestants by conducting and analyzing field and lab experiments.

PhD Student in Global Health and Population, Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
email jgoldberg1@g.harvard.edu

Jonah Goldberg is a PhD student in the Department of Global Health and Population at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. He specializes in the study of health systems, and his research seeks to apply insights from organizational economics to better understand variation in healthcare provider performance in low- and middle-income countries, especially in the public sector. Before starting his PhD, Jonah worked in research at Harvard Chan and, previously, at Harvard Business School. He holds an AB in International Relations (Political Economy) from Brown University.

item.title: John Reynolds
item.description:

PhD Student for Public Policy, Harvard Kennedy School
