laptop_windows Personal Website

John is a graduate student studying for his PhD in Public Policy at the Harvard Kennedy School, with a focus on Politics and Institutions. He is also a Captain in the U.S. Army. His research interests are bureaucracy, American political institutions, and civil service behavior. For my dissertation I intend to examine how the federal bureaucracy would work to ensure a domestic food supply during a large-scale world war.

PhD Candidate in Economics, University of California, Berkeley

Joan Martinez is a PhD candidate in Economics at the University of California Berkeley. She works on topics in labor, education economics, and applied econometrics. She received her MSc degree in Economics at University College London and BA at Universidad del Pacífico in Peru. She received a Chevening Award from the Foreign, Commonwealth, and Development Office (FCDO) from the United Kingdom. Prior to her doctoral studies, she worked as a research assistant at Universidad del Pacífico and an evaluation specialist in the Ministry of Economics and Finance.

Jiyeon Lee is an M.A. student in the RSEA program at Harvard, with a disciplinary focus on sociology. Her research interests broadly concern social boundaries and belonging-making processes, as well as how these are interactively negotiated and relationally maintained by individual and institutional actors. Her recent work explores the gendered processes of immigrant integration in the neoliberal welfare state.

item.title: Jing Ling Tan
item.description:
Jing Ling Tan

PhD Student, Department of Government, GSAS
email jingling_tan@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Jing Ling Tan is a PhD student in the Department of Government at Harvard University. His research examines the linkages between international politics and domestic environmental politics/policies. This includes how the international political economy, institutions, and global norms influence political behavior on climate change and vice versa. He holds a Master’s in International Public Affairs from the University of Wisconsin-Madison and a dual bachelor’s in political science from Sciences Po and the National University of Singapore.

item.title: Jihwan Jeong
item.description:
Jihwan Jeong

PhD Student, Department of Government, GSAS
email jihwanjeong@fas.harvard.edu

My research focuses on the interplay between economic statecraft and the political landscape within the targeted state. I posit that this relationship is both reciprocal and mutable, indicating that the deployment of economic statecraft can indeed drive political transformation. My goal is to enrich the discourse on economic statecraft by shedding light on its subnational political impacts. Specifically, I aim to elucidate the conditions that enhance the effectiveness of economic statecraft.

item.title: Jialu Li
item.description:
Jialu Li

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email jialu_li@g.harvard.edu

Jialu Li is a PhD student in the Department of Government at Harvard University. Her research focuses on comparative political economy, political development, and state-business relationship, especially around how states interact with foreign firms. Jialu holds a B.A. in Political Science from Peking University and an M.A. in Regional Studies–East Asia from Harvard University.

Jan Kinne

CGA Visiting Fellow
email jan.kinne@istari.ai
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Jan Kinne is a postdoc in the research area "Economics of Innovation and Business Dynamics" at the Center for European Economic Research (ZEW Mannheim). He studied geoinformatics at the University of Heidelberg and received his PhD from the University of Salzburg on the topic of "web-based innovation indicators for microgeographic analyses". He continues to conduct methodological research around the use of (text-based) web data for innovation research. He is also the co-founder and CEO of ISTARI.AI. The company specializes in AI-based analysis of enterprise web data.

item.title: James Sappenfield
item.description:
James Sappenfield

Doctoral Candidate in Technology and Operations Management at Harvard Business School
email jsappenfield@hbs.edu
laptop_windows LISH
James Sappenfield is a doctoral student at Harvard Business School in the Technology and Operations Management (TOM) unit and also an affiliate researcher in the Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard. His current projects focus on the evaluation of novel ideas in managerial and scientific settings, and how such evaluation drives the direction of innovative search. More broadly, his research interests lie in the economics of innovation, collaboration, crowdsourcing, and aggregate decision making, especially as they apply to the generation of ideas and new knowledge in science. Prior to attending HBS, he previously worked as a research assistant at MIT - Sloan School of Management, the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER), and Boston University, and taught high school mathematics with Dallas ISD as a member of Teach For America. He holds a BA in Political Science and a BS in Business Administration, both from Boston University.

item.title: James D Myers
item.description:
James D Myers

Developer, Dataverse Core Development Team
Senior Developer and Architect for the Global Dataverse Community Consortium (GDCC)
email jim.myers@computer.org
laptop_windows Website

Dr. Myers is an independent software architect, designer, and developer currently working in the Dataverse community through the Global Dataverse Community Consortium (GDCC) and the Qualitative Data Repository (QDR). He received his B.A. in Physics from Cornell University (1985) and his Ph.D. in Chemistry from the University of California at Berkeley (1993). He has more than three decades of experience in the development and deployment of Cyberinfrastructure for research, education, and industrial application and has participated in the planning and execution of multiple large community cyberinfrastructure projects for the US NSF, ONR, and DOE. Dr. Myers has been active in the development of provenance and content management standards and has led efforts to design and develop data-intensive hardware and software systems. As a co-PI on SEAD DataNet project, Dr. Myers helped develop a scalable mechanism for the long-term preservation of scientific research results based on semantic web and cloud technologies and leveraging active and social curation approaches to increase data re-use and lower lifecycle costs. He has development experience using multiple languages (Java, Scala, PHP, Javascript, C/C++) and technologies (including relational and document databases, semantic web, preservation formats, dev/Ops, Docker, AWS).

Jake Green is from Knoxville, Tennessee and is a proud product of Knox County Public Schools. He has worked in campaigns, in policy, and in a bowling alley, and brings an ethos of efficacy and the capacity for change to his professional and academic work alike.

item.title: Jahred Liddie
item.description:
Jahred Liddie

PhD Candidate, Population Health Sciences, Environmental Health Department, HSPH
email jliddie@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Jahred is a PhD candidate in the Population Health Sciences program at HSPH. He is a student in the Department of Environmental Health. He studies per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), which are a ubiquitous and often persistent group of chemical contaminants. Specifically, he is interested in assessing human exposure to PFAS, the environmental justice ramifications of PFAS pollution, and impacts on human health. Prior to starting graduate school, he was a sustainability consultant at Sphera (formerly thinkstep), where he primarily conducted environmental life cycle assessments. He received his B.A. in Environmental Sciences and Engineering from Harvard College and his S.M. in Environmental Health from the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health.

item.title: Heidi R Legg
item.description:
Heidi R Legg

IQSS Affiliate
Former Fellow, Future of Media Project
laptop_windows The Future of Media Project
laptop_windows Twitter

Heidi Legg was formerly the Future of Media fellow at the Institute of Quantitative Social Science at Harvard 2020-2022 where she indexed the entire US and Canadian media system with extensive ownership details on every outlet. By mapping the commercial and non-profit US Media ecosystems she has been able to offer informed solutions on improving transparency and trust in news. Her index work was published in mainstream media Axios, USA Today, Nieman Labs, Columbia Journalism Review, and the UK’s Press-Gazette. Heidi wrote sweeping white papers while at the Shorenstein Center from 2018-2020 including The Fight Against Disinformation in the US, A Landscape Study of Local News Across America and Preserving America’s Thought Leader Magazines. She was the founder of TheEditorial.com. She is mission driven to ensure more people have access to quality knowledge and that journalism survives with its own revenue model. She also published numerous OpEds on local news models for CNN and the Boston Globe, on section 230 for both the Boston Globe and new Capital Hill Citizen.

Born in Moncton, New Brunswick, she holds a Graduate degree in Journalism from Concordia University, Montreal (1994) and a Bachelor of Communications, Mount Saint Vincent University, Halifax (1993), with a minor in History from Dalhousie University (thesis on Marshall McLuhan, Canadian media theorist).

item.title: Harry Oppenheimer
item.description:
Harry Oppenheimer

Government, GSAS
email hoppenheimer@g.harvard.edu
I am a second year PhD student in the Department of Government with a focus on international relations, techNology, and conflict. My research focuses on how techNological advancement impacts traditional international relations theories and provides new opportunities to test conventional hypotheses. This includes threat perception and assessment, coercive diplomacy, and the roll of the media and public opinion in national security. Prior to beginning my PhD, I was was a research associate for national security at the Council on Foreign Relations. I received my BA in International Relations from New York University in 2014.

item.description:
Hanna Katz

Graduate Student, Department of Sociology, GSAS
email hkatz@g.harvard.edu

Hanna is a PhD student in Sociology. Originally from New Haven, CT, she graduated from Princeton in 2011 with a concentration in Sociology and a certificate in American Studies.
As an undergraduate, she conducted quantitative research that examined the ways in which a single police stop – even in the absence of clear evidence of wrongdoing – can diminish young people’s sense of control over their own lives. Following graduation, Hanna put this research to practice at two New York-based nonprofit organizations, where she provided direct legal and social services to court-involved youth.

item.title: Georgia Kernell
item.description:
Georgia Kernell

Postdoctoral Fellow at University of Pennsylvania
Georgia Kernell successfully defended her PhD dissertation in political science at Columbia University in September 2008. Her research interests include political parties, methodology, behavior and comparative institutions. Her dissertation, "Political Party Organizations and Representation: Party Responsiveness, Candidate Heterogeneity and Voter Behavior in Mature Parliamentary Systems," examines how a party's organization--especially its decentralization with respect to candidate selection, resource allocation, and recruitment of delegates to the national party convention--shapes its electoral appeal and constituent support. The thesis uses an original data set of party institutions, party statutes, and interviews for 66 parties in 20 mature parliamentary democracies. Georgia also studies quantitative methods, and has two publications in Political Analysis. Currently, Georgia is working on several projects that examine the institutions that regulate party diversity, the normative implications of party organizations for representation, and the information strategies that affect consumers' economic forecasts. During the spring she will teach a freshman seminar on "Comparative Political Parties and Party Systems" at Penn, where she is a postdoctoral fellow in Penn's Program on Democracy, Citizenship, and Constitutionalism.

item.title: Felipe Jordan
item.description:
Felipe Jordan

Economics, Harvard Kennedy School
email fjordancolzani@g.harvard.edu

My research focuses on the political economy of sustainable development. Specifically, I am interested in how institutions interact with local communities’ cultural and historical background to determine economic wellbeing and environmental quality. My current work focuses on events in Latin America’s history that offer quasi-experimental variation to empirically assess the effects of communal property rights systems, economic inequality, and fractionalization on sustainable development. My research expands to other areas within political economy and environmental economics, such as the agency problem within public bureaucracies and health impacts from exposure to air pollution.

PhD Candidate in Education Policy and Program Evaluation, Graduate School of Education
email farahmallah@g.harvard.edu

Farah Mallah is a PhD student in Education & Policy Evaluation. Her research focuses on identifying how external factors including financial constraints, discrimination and migration prospects contribute to inequities in education outcomes, and how best to address them. Prior to her doctoral studies, Farah worked as a Research Analyst in the Education and Crime Labs at UChicago. Farah holds a BSc in International Economics from Georgetown University and an EdM in International Education Policy from Harvard.

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, GSAS

Emma's research interests lie at the intersection of development and behavioral economics. Her current work focuses on understanding the role of social learning in eroding stigma and increasing mental health care-seeking among Syrian refugees in Jordan. laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter

 

"Emma Rackstraw is a PhD candidate in Public Policy (Economics Track) at Harvard. Her research is focused on understanding the decision-making processes and incentive systems facing key decision-makers in the U.S. criminal justice system and labor market, as well as how those decisions interact with systemic inequities. Her work uses theory and techniques from labor economics, experimental economics, and behavioral economics, as well as other disciplines like psychology and political science. She is a NSF GRFP Fellow.

Elysia Larson is a doctoral student in the Department of Global Health and Population at the Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health. Elysia's research interests focus on improving health system quality and responsiveness in low and middle income countries. Her research explores the ways in which different stakeholders in the health system, particularly communities, define and influence health system quality. Elysia uses epidemiologic and ecoNometric methods to evaluate health systems interventions and reforms.Elysia received her MPH in epidemiology and international health from Boston University and her BSc in biology and mathematics from McGill University. She currently manages a NIH-funded, cluster-randomized maternal and newborn health study in Pwani Region, Tanzania (PI: Margaret Kruk) and has been involved in field work in Tanzania, Kenya, Zambia, and South Africa. Website.

item.title: Elizabeth Thom
item.description:
Elizabeth Thom

PhD Candidate, Government and Social Policy Program
Malcolm Hewitt Wiener PhD Scholar in Poverty and Justice, Multidisciplinary Program in Inequality & Social Policy
email ethom@g.harvard.edu

Elizabeth Thom is a PhD candidate in Government and Social Policy. Her research focuses on the politics of the American welfare state, federalism, political economy and political geography. She utilizes quantitative methods to map the spatial dimensions of welfare state dependence and political attitudes.

item.title: Elisabeth Stockinger
item.description:
Elisabeth Stockinger

Doctoral candidate at ETH Zurich
email elisabeth.stockinger@live.at
laptop_windows Website

Elisabeth works at the intersection of democracy and technology, focusing on the socio-technical dynamics that influence human behaviour, societal structures, and democratic processes. She combines data-driven, model-based and qualitative methods to address critical issues such as misinformation on social media, the design of public interest technology, and societal value dynamics. Currently, she explores on the potential of generative AI to support democratic deliberative processes.

item.title: Eleanor Krause
item.description:
Eleanor Krause

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, GSAS
email eleanorkrause@fas.harvard.edu

Eleanor is a PhD candidate in Public Policy at the Harvard Kennedy School. Her research considers the intersection of place, environment, and economic inequality. She is particularly interested in understanding how the characteristics of local labor markets determine how individuals and communities adjust to economic and environmental change. Drawing from labor, urban, and environmental economics, her research advances our understanding of the economics of place, and helps inform how policymakers might confront deepening spatial inequality. Prior to her doctoral studies, Eleanor worked at the Brookings Institution and the World Resources Institute.

item.title: Edward Ngai
item.description:
Edward Ngai

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email ngai@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Ed is a PhD candidate in the Department of Government. He studies the effects of democratic transition on political attitudes. He is interested in democratization, the political economy of redistribution, and Chinese politics. He has a B.A. from Stanford (2015) and an M.A. from Peking University (2017).

Dylan Nguyen

PhD Student in Sociology and Social Policy, Kennedy School of Government
email dnguyen@hks.harvard.edu

Dylan is broadly interested in race, space, and place. They aim to understand how demographic processes like immigration interact with neighborhood change and development to shape the communities and places we call home.

item.description:
Drew Johnston

Research Associate, Opportunity Insights
email drewjohnston@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
Drew Johnston is a Research Associate at Opportunity Insights. His research interests are in urban economics, labor economics, and political economy. He is particularly interested in the way social networks interact with the above fields, and in the ways in which new sources of data can be used to address questions in these fields.

item.title: Dorothy Manevich
item.description:
Dorothy Manevich

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email dmanevich@g.harvard.edu

Dorothy is a PhD candidate in the Department of Government interested in political psychology and political behavior. Her work employs both quantitative and qualitative methods, with a particular emphasis on experiments and surveys. Dorothy’s dissertation investigates the psychological and social roots of political activism. Prior to joining the Government Department, Dorothy worked as a campaign and issue advocacy pollster. She is active in the Harvard Graduate Student Union (UAW) and Massachusetts state politics.

item.title: Dorit Talia Stein
item.description:
Dorit Talia Stein

PhD Candidate in Global Health Systems, TH Chan School of Public Health
email doritstein@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Twitter

Dorit is in the Health Systems track of the Population Health Sciences PhD within the Global Health and Population Department at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. She is a health systems and policy researcher with interests in health systems performance, health financing, and health systems governance in decentralized contexts. Her ongoing research work applies decision science, economic, and epidemiological methods to model health, financial risk protection, and equity impacts of improving health systems performance for managing chronic diseases to inform health policy, planning, and priority setting at global and local levels. Dorit received her Master of Science from HSPH in 2019 and Bachelor of Science in Human Biology and Society with a minor in Public Affairs from UCLA in 2014. She grew up in San Diego, California and loves surfing, swimming, scuba diving, and generally being outside.

item.title: Don Sizemore, II
item.description:
Don Sizemore, II

Developer, Dataverse Core Development Team
Systems Programmer, HW Odum Institute, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
email don.sizemore@gmail.com
laptop_windows Website

Don Sizemore became involved with IQSS through the Dataverse project when he joined the Odum Institute in 2015. He is the primary author of the Dataverse-Ansible role, a consultant through the Global Dataverse Community Consortium, and attempts to support the Dataverse community over its various communication channels. He holds a Master of Information Science from UNC's School of Information and Library Science, but highly cherishes his undergraduate minor in music performance on pipe organ.

item.title: Dilan Tulan
item.description:
Dilan Tulan

PhD Student in Public Policy, HKS
email dilan_tulan@fas.harvard.edu

Dilan’s research focuses on the psychology of conflict and collaboration, particularly how disagreement shapes group dynamics, judgment, and decision-making. She is especially interested in how advocates are selected, the psychology of conflict aversion, and interventions aimed at reducing affective polarization.

item.title: Diego Santa María Alcázar
item.description:
Diego Santa María Alcázar

PhD student (4th year) in Economics, Graduate School of Arts and Sciences
email dsantamaria@g.harvard.edu

Diego studies development economics, with secondary interests in urban economics, trade, and industrial organization. His current projects aim to understand the structure of food distribution networks and the interaction between the informal and formal retail sectors in urban settings. While most of his work focuses on Latin America, he is open to studying similar themes in other regions.

item.title: Devon Rojas
item.description:
Devon Rojas

PhD Student in Sociology, GSAS
email drojas@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Devon Rojas is a PhD student in Sociology at Harvard. In his research, he aims to build deeper understandings of LGBTQ+ status and meaning within the workplace and beyond. He is interested in leveraging text data and natural language processing (NLP) techniques to draw out cultural meaning, and using this to explore the role of culture in shaping organizational inequality. He also intends to investigate the interplay of organizations and social movements in shaping inequality broadly. He hopes that his research will contribute to a greater awareness and understanding of the unique challenges that LGBTQ+ individuals encounter, and the development of organizational tools and policies to combat LGBTQ+ discrimination.

David Romney

Harvard University, Graduate School of Arts and Sciences, Department of Government

I am a Ph.D. student in the Department of Government at Harvard University. My substantive research interests lie in the psychology of intergroup relations and ethnic conflict, I am regionally interested in the Middle East and Southeast Asia, and my methodological interests include experimental methods and text analysis. I hold a degree in Middle East Studies and Arabic Language (B.A.) from Brigham Young University.

item.title: Dave Wang
item.description:
Dave Wang

AI Research Fellow
Co-Founder of 99 Capital
email dave_wang@alumni.harvard.edu
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Dave is the co-founder of 99 Capital, a venture fund investing in frontier technology and markets. Before that, he led technical investments across blockchain & fintech for SoftBank's $5B Latin America Fund. He has deep research interests in AI / ML applications particularly in binary outcome events like survey markets, where he is working with Prof. Chase Harrison on research at the IQSS in this area. Dave earned his BA in Statistics from Harvard, Cum Laude with a thesis on AI / ML applications on predictive survey methodology.

item.title: Daoye Zhu
item.description:
Daoye Zhu

Research Associate, Center for Geographic Analysis
email daoyezhu@fas.harvard.edu

Daoye Zhu is a Research Associate at the Center for Geographic Analysis (CGA) of Harvard University. He obtained his PhD in Data Science (Computer Science and Technology) from the Center for Data Science of Peking University in 2022. He was a Visiting Scholar in the Lab of Interdisciplinary Spatial Analysis (LISA Lab) at the University of Cambridge from 2020 to 2021 and a Visiting Fellow at the CGA of Harvard University from 2021 to 2022. As a Research Associate at the CGA, he collaborates with Devika Kakkar and other CGA staff, with supervision from Prof. S.V. Subramanian. His research interests mainly include geospatial big data, GeoAI applications, and geographic information engineering.

item.title: Daniel Stuart
item.description:
Daniel Stuart

Public Policy, Harvard Kennedy School
email dstuart1@g.harvard.edu
Daniel is a PhD Candidate in Public Policy at Harvard University. His research interests are in environmental ecoNomics, public ecoNomics, and labor ecoNomics. His current research includes a project on the local ecoNomic and fiscal impacts of refugee resettlement in the United States. Prior to his Ph.D. studies, he worked as a research assistant at the Massachusetts Institute of TechNology and as a Pre-Doctoral Fellow at the Energy Policy Institute at Chicago. Daniel received a B.A. in Economics with HoNors from Swarthmore College.

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email dlowery@g.harvard.edu

Daniel Lowery is a graduate student in the Department of Government.  His research agenda is at the intersection of historical political economy, autocracy, and development. In particular, he interested in the rise, survival, and fall of autocratic rulers and institutions, long-run development and historical legacies, inequality, and conflict.

GIS Research Fellow, Center for Geographic Analysis
email joel.cuatianquis@gssi.it
laptop_windows Personal Website
  • Linkedin

Crhistian Joel González-Cuatianquis is a PhD candidate in Regional Science and Economic Geography at the Gran Sasso Science Institute (GSSI), with a background in economics. He previously worked at Mexico’s National Council for the Evaluation of Social Development Policy (CONEVAL), where he coordinated research and reports on social rights, the use of evaluations, and subnational social development policies.

item.title: Craig Marris
item.description:
Craig Marris

Executive Affiliate, Startup Foundry
Co-founder and Co-CEO, SeeDigital.ai
email craigmarris@fas.harvard.edu
  • Linkedin

Craig Marris is an Executive Affiliate at IQSS, where he mentors startups developing applied AI and data science solutions with societal impact. He is the co-founder and Co-CEO of SeeDigital.ai, a company using AI-powered CCTV analytics and agentic automation to elevate workplace safety and operational compliance. Craig is also the co-founder of Coretex, a global telematics and compliance platform that he helped scale from inception to its successful $188 million acquisition by EROAD. With senior executive experience spanning technology, sustainability, transportation, and AI-enabled operations, he brings strategic insight and hands-on leadership to innovators navigating the intersection of data science and real-world problem-solving.

item.title: Connie Xu
item.description:
Connie Xu

PhD Candidate in Health Policy
email conniexu@g.harvard.edu

Connie Xu is a PhD candidate in the economics track of the health policy program. Her research interests sit at the intersection of labor and health economics, with a focus on the scientific innovation in the life sciences. During her PhD, her projects have investigated productivity variation in fundamental life-sciences discoveries and the role of scientific equipment in shaping research outcomes. Prior to joining Harvard, Connie worked as a predoctoral fellow at MIT and holds bachelor's degrees in Economics and Statistics from the University of Chicago.

item.description:
Clemens Havas

Visiting Fellow, CGA
email clemensrudolf.havas@sbg.ac.at

Clemens Havas is a PhD candidate at University of Salzburg’s Department of Geoinformatics – Z_GIS with a background in computer science. He specializes in combining state-of-the-art machine learning algorithms with geospatial analysis to extract information about large-scale events like a natural disaster. The information is provided in near real-time after the outbreak of an event by utilizing new data sources like social media networks.

item.title: Christopher T. Kenny
item.description:
Christopher T. Kenny

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email christopherkenny@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter
Chris is a PhD Candidate in the Government Department. He studies American Politics and Political Methodology. Substantively, he focuses on legislative redistricting and electoral reform. Methodologically, Chris is interested in redistricting simulation algorithms, small area estimation, and ecological inference. His prior research looks at validating simulation methodologies and the effects of privacy protections on Census data. He is the coauthor of several R packages, notably redist, geomander, and PL94171.

Before coming to Harvard, Chris earned a BA in Mathematics and Government from Cornell University. He is a member of the Algorithm-Assisted Redistricting Methodology Project and is also affiliated with the Center for American Political Studies at Harvard University.

item.title: Chris Felton
item.description:
Chris Felton

Postdoctoral Fellow in Education, GSE
email christopher_felton@gse.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Twitter

Chris's work develops and showcases methods for drawing causal conclusions from observational data when identification assumptions are suspect. He is particularly interested in applying these methods to sociological questions related to suicide and mental health in the US. In the past, he has worked on instrumental variables methods and the interrupted time series design. He is currently working on panel data methods for causal inference with Luke Miratrix.

item.title: Chinemelu Okafor
item.description:
Chinemelu Okafor

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
Karl Deutsch Fellow, Graduate Prize Fellow
Graduate Student Affiliate, Weatherhead Center for International Affairs
email cokafor@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Chinemelu Okafor is a researcher and scholar originating from Umuoji, Anambra State, Nigeria. Currently, she is a doctoral student, Graduate Prize Fellow, and Karl Deutsch Fellow in Harvard’s Government Department. Chinemelu seeks to confront pressing economic policy issues in West Africa by understanding and explaining the institutional (formal and informal), structural (network), economic, and electoral incentives that can work to constrain political elite behavior in weakly institutionalized settings. Chinemelu is also a Graduate Student Affiliate with Harvard’s Weatherhead Center for International Affairs and with Harvard’s Institute for Quantitative Social Sciences. Furthermore, she concurrently works at the Founder and President of the Research in Color Foundation, a 501c(3) non-profit organization which seeks to increase the number (and improve the retention) of historically excluded scholars in economics and economics-adjacent fields through mentorship and financial support.

item.title: Chika Okafor
item.description:
Chika Okafor

Postdoctoral Fellow, National Bureau of Economic Research
email coo@alumni.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

As an economist and a lawyer with diverse policy expertise, Chika Okafor uses the tools of economics, law, and public policy to explore foundational matters of justice—with recent research spanning economic inequality, criminal justice, and climate change. Methodologically, his scholarship integrates economic theory, empirical techniques, and legal analysis to uncover mechanisms of—as well as inform solutions to—major economic and social problems impacting contemporary American society.

Okafor earned his PhD in economics from Harvard University, his JD from Yale Law School, and his BA from Stanford University.

item.title: Channing Spencer
item.description:
Channing Spencer

Organizational Behavior & Sociology, HBS and GSAS
email cspencer@hbs.edu

Channing Spencer is a Doctoral Student in the Organizational Behavior program jointly offered by Harvard Business School and the Department of Sociology at Harvard. Her research focuses on inequality (gender & race), diversity & inclusion, and labor market & workplace dynamics. Channing holds an A.B. from Harvard, where she wrote her undergraduate thesis on the economic, social, and political status of domestic workers in Brazil and employer-employee relations in the informal labor market. Prior to her doctoral studies, she worked at Microsoft and Pearson PLC.

item.title: Changzheng Yuan
item.description:
Changzheng Yuan

Harvard School of Public Health, Department of Nutrition
email Chy478@mail.harvard.edu
I am a second year doctoral student in Nutritional Epidemiology at HSPH. I also earned a ScM degree in Epidemiology at HSPH. My research interest focuses on investigating effective and efficient interventions to promote health behavior, including physical activity and healthy eating, and targeting at individual factors as well as social factors, including built environment and information environment, especially in developing countries. I also looked at peer effect on health behaviors among college students, with an aim to investigate how to utilize peer effect to design health behavior interventions.

item.description:
Brian Guay

Postdoctoral Researcher, Department of Political Science & Sloan School of Management, Massachusetts Institute of Technology
email brianmguay@gmail.com
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter

Brian Guay is a postdoctoral researcher at MIT in the Department of Political Science & Sloan School of Management. Brian’s research is at the intersection of political behavior and methodology, with a specific focus on misinformation and polarization. His work draws on theories from cognitive and social psychology and use a wide range of methods, including field experiments, surveys, and natural language processing with social media data. He received his PhD from the Department of Political Science at Duke University in the spring of 2021, where he was a member of the Duke Polarization Lab and Duke Initiative on Survey Methodology.

item.title: Brad Rosen
item.description:
Brad Rosen

Entrepreneur-In-Residence (EIR), IQSS Startup Foundry
email bradrosen@iq.harvard.edu
  • Linkedin

Brad Rosen is a serial entrepreneur and technology executive with deep experience building and scaling data-driven companies at the intersection of AI, software, and applied research. He is a co-founder and former COO of NODAR, a 3D vision company advancing perception systems for autonomous vehicles, and has led or founded multiple venture-backed companies across enterprise and consumer markets, with successful exits to strategic acquirers including Cisco, Motorola, and others. Brad holds a BS in Electrical Engineering from the University of Colorado and an MBA from MIT Sloan. At the Institute for Quantitative Social Science (IQSS) Startup Foundry, he focuses on helping translate cutting-edge research in data science, causal inference, and AI into commercially viable products and impactful real-world applications.

Sociology, GSAS

Her work has also been supported by Harvard's Center on the Developing Child, the Center for American Political Studies (CAPS), the Weatherhead Center, and the Tobin Project. item.title: Babak Fotouhi
item.description:
Babak Fotouhi

JSMF Post-doctoral Fellow at the Program for Evolutionary Dynamics, Harvard University

Babak employs concepts from sociology, behavioral economics, and network science to study human behavior and interaction theoretically and experimentally. Broadly, he studies the linkages and mutual influences of two categories of entities. The first one subsumes social structure, social context, and culture. The second one encompasses perceptions, preferences, behaviors, and interactions. A central strand of his research revolves around human cooperation. It attends to questions such as how altruism, prosociality, and trust, can be promoted to outweigh egoism, what factors strengthen or impair trust, and what is the interplay between social networks and individual cooperative attitudes. Babak received his PhD from the department of electrical and computer engineering at McGill university, after receiving an MSc in physics from Perimeter Institute for Theoretical Physics. He worked on the mathematics and statistical physics of networks---e.g., evolution of the structure of complex networks (social, biological, and technological), and diffusion of information, influence, and pathogens on social networks. After his PhD, he received an MA in Sociology from McGill. His main focus was quantitative methods and computational social sciences (linking machine learning and data science to sociology). His other interests in sociology are social theory and social psychology. On the methodological side, his main interest is causal inference, particularly for networked data.

item.description:
Averell Schmidt

PhD Candidate in Public Policy, Harvard Kennedy School
email aschmidt@g.harvard.edu
I am PhD candidate in public policy at the Harvard Kennedy School. I research international relations with a focus on the intersection of national security, human rights, and diplomacy. I am broadly interested in how states learn from and influence one another. Before beginning my doctoral studies, I served with the Peace Corps in Morocco, fought forest fires in the Sawtooth National Forest, and worked for public policy research organizations in Sri Lanka, Israel, Georgia, and Egypt. I hold an MPP from the Harvard Kennedy School and a BA from Lewis & Clark College.

item.title: Aurore Gonzalez
item.description:
Aurore Gonzalez

Linguistics, GSAS
email auroregonzalez@g.harvard.edu
My main research interests are related to formal semantics, pragmatics and the syntax-semantics interface. I am also interested in understanding how experimental investigations shed new light on linguistic topics such as countability distinctions, the interpretation of disjunction etc. In my recent work, I have investigated the role played by language in quantity judgment tasks (Who has more NoUN?, Barner & Snedeker 2005), and in particular the role played by linguistic cues to atomicity by conducting online experiments in English and in French.

item.title: Audrey Feldman
item.description:
Audrey Feldman

PhD Student in Public Policy, HKS
email audreyfeldman@g.harvard.edu

Audrey Feldman is a PhD student in Public Policy at the Harvard Kennedy School on the Judgement and Decision-Making track. As a research fellow at The People Lab, she supports collaborations with state and local governments to generate rigorous evidence and implement field experiments. She investigates how policy implementation, take-up, and efficacy can be improved using human behavior-informed experimental and economic methods. She is interested in improving trust, transparency, and communication between citizens and their governments.

item.title: Arkādijs Zvaigzne
item.description:
Arkādijs Zvaigzne

PhD Candidate in Education, GSE
email azvaigzne@fas.harvard.edu

Arkadijs Zvaigzne is a PhD student in Education. He is interested in the ways that higher education can be used to decrease inequality and promote development. His research uses econometric and machine learning approaches to estimate the role of the supply side of higher education – the behavior of colleges and universities – in helping to fulfill the promise and goals of higher education. Prior to Harvard, he has worked in educational policy and administration in Latvia. Arkadijs earned a M.S.Ed. in Higher Education from the University of Pennsylvania in 2020.

item.title: Ariella Kirstal
item.description:
Ariella Kirstal

Organizational Behavior, Harvard Business School
email akristal@hbs.edu
Ariella is a doctoral student in Organizational Behavior at Harvard Business School where she studies behavioral design. Specifically, she is interested in how environments can be structured to reduce bias in the workplace, in educational settings, in the online context, and in many others. In particular she is interested in using big data methods, such as computational text analysis. Previously, Ariella worked for the Behavioural Insights Team in London, where she applied findings from behavioral science to public policy. She has focused on delivering policy advice and designing and implementing field experiments related to sustainable transportation, criminal justice, and organizational behavior. Ariella graduated from Yale University with a BA in Ethics, Politics, and Economics.

item.title: Anna Gifty Opoku
item.description:
Anna Gifty Opoku-Agyeman

Affiliated Graduate Student
laptop_windows Website
Anna Gifty Opoku-Agyeman is doctoral candidate at Harvard Kennedy School studying public policy and economics. She is a doctoral fellow for the National Science Foundation, Ford Foundation, and the Stone Program in Wealth Distribution, Inequality & Social Policy. She is a former research fellow of Harvard Kennedy School’s Women and Public Policy Center. Her research aims to improve how talent is recruited, retained, and assessed in workplace settings.

In 2022, she published a critically acclaimed collection, The Black Agenda: Bold Solutions for a Broken System, which is the first trade publication to exclusively feature Black scholars and experts across economics, education, health, climate, criminal justice, and technology. In 2019, she co-founded the viral and award-winning digital campaign #BlackBirdersWeek. The year prior she also co-founded The Sadie Collective, the first non-profit organization to address the underrepresentation of Black women in economics, finance, and policy. To date, she remains the youngest recipient for a CEDAW Women's Rights Award by the United Nations Convention on the Elimination all forms of Discrimination Against Women— previously awarded to Vice President Kamala Harris and former Democratic House Speaker Nancy Pelosi. In 2023, she achieved the distinction of being chosen for the inaugural Forbes 30 under 30 cohort in Boston. Her writing and commentary is featured widely by media outlets such as TIME, Bloomberg, NPR, and The New York Times.

item.title: Ankana Saha
item.description:
Ankana Saha

PhD Student in Linguistics, GSAS
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Ankana is a PhD student in the Department of Linguistics studying natural language semantics. She uses tools of formal mathematics and logic to study how utterances come to have the meanings they do, and how meaning interacts with linguistic structure and discourse contexts. She is particularly interested in investigating issues pertaining to the nominal domain like the semantics of bare nouns in languages with and without determiners, kind reference, number marking, definiteness, demonstratives, anaphora, and the cross-linguistic variability in the expression of epistemic indefinites. She explores these issues using a mix of formal theory, experimental methods, and fieldwork, with an empirical focus on South Asian languages. In her current collaborative project, she uses experimental and quantitative methodology to investigate the behavior of definite descriptions and demonstrative expressions in anaphoric contexts in order to understand the core building blocks of definiteness in Language.

item.title: Angela Lee
item.description:
Angela Lee

IQSS Affiliate
Research Scientist, Facebook
email angelawlee1@gmail.com
laptop_windows Website

Angela Lee completed a PhD in sociology at Harvard University in 2021. She is currently a Research Scientist at Facebook.

Her dissertation research examined wealth inequality by gender in the United States. She looked at trends in the gender wealth gap over the past three decades, changes in the gender wealth gap over the life course, and wealth inequality between husbands and wives. She has also studied crime and criminal justice, including the causal effect of employment on crime for juvenile offenders and the high rates of dropout in residential drug treatment.

item.title: Andrea Neyra Nazarrett
item.description:
Andrea Neyra Nazarrett

PhD Candidate in Economics, GSAS
email aneyra@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Twitter
laptop_windows LinkedIn

Andrea is a PhD student in economics in the Political Economy and Government Program at Harvard University. She is interested in studying the role of labor markets on labor poverty and inequality. More specifically, how the fissuring of labor market institutions affects workers’ living conditions.  She also has ongoing work in personnel economics investigating how labor management inside firms can affect regional labor markets. Before the PhD, Andrea worked at the World Bank and the InterAmerican Development Bank focusing on development in Latin America and the Caribbean. Andrea holds a Master's in Public Policy from Harvard Kennedy School and a BA in Economics from ITAM in Mexico.

item.title: Andrea Nasuto
item.description:
Andrea Nasuto

Associate of IQSS
PhD Candidate, University of Liverpool
email anasuto@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows GitHub
laptop_windows Google Scholar citations
laptop_windows Twitter/X

Andrea Nasuto is a PhD candidate in Geographic Data Science at the University of Liverpool. His research combines large language models, geospatial modeling, and machine learning to analyze human behavior, with a focus on immigration sentiment and human mobility. His PhD examines immigration sentiment both online and offline using social media data, LLMs, and geospatial modeling. He has worked with multiple UN agencies on projects related to migrant displacement, crisis impacts, and human mobility in Ukraine and Latin America. Andrea has also contributed to studies and reports on urban sciences and climate-driven market shifts. His work has been cited in various research publications. He has been invited to present his research at multiple institutions, including MIT, Brown University, the Berkman Klein Center at Harvard, the United Nations Population & Development Division in New York, and the International Organization for Migration (IOM) in London.

item.title: Andrea Feigl
item.description:
Andrea Feigl

Graduate Student, Harvard School of Public Health
email afeigl@hsph.harvard.edu
Prior to embarking on her doctoral studies at Harvard University, Andrea had a broad exposure to global health policy and research. She previously worked at the Family and Community Health Unit at PAHO, where she was involved in the evaluation of an IMCI (Integrated Management for Childhood Illness) project in Ecuador, Paraguay, and Peru. She also worked for one year for the Canadian Institutes of Health Research, for the Institute for Nutrition, Metabolism, and Diabetes. Most recently, she completed a working paper on Development Aid Flows for Chronic Diseases, for the Center for Global Development. She also co-authored a background paper on the political ecoNomy of universal healthcare for the WHO., and a WEF/HSPH report on the global ecoNomic burden of NCDs.Her current research interests include studying factors that enable countries to effectively implement universal healthcare, as well as cost-effectiveness of chronic disease interventions. She is a Steering Committe member of the Young Professionals for Chronic Diseases, an advocacy group to help promote the issue of chronic diseases on the development agenda.

item.title: Andi Zhou
item.description:
Andi Zhou

PhD Candidate, Department of Government
email azhou@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter

Andi is a Ph.D. candidate in the Department of Government. His research examines the psychological roots of interstate territorial conflict. Andi's projects use original survey data and experimental methods to investigate why people care about territorial disputes and how uncompromising attitudes may be overcome to resolve them. Previously, Andi was a program coordinator at the EastWest Institute facilitating unofficial U.S.-China and U.S.-Russia diplomatic convenings. He has also worked with the U.S. Department of Labor as a Harold W. Rosenthal Fellow. His op-eds on foreign policy have appeared in The Diplomat and RealClearDefense. Andi received a B.A. in Global Affairs from Yale University and an MPA from the School of Public and International Affairs at Princeton University.

Amir Mahdavi is an AM candidate studying Middle East politics at Harvard University. He earned a Master`s degree in Conflict Resolution from Brandeis University, and he accepted a grant for best research in the field of negotiation from Harvard Law School. Amir has published three Middle Eastern Briefs at Brandeis along with op-eds in Guardian, Washington Post, and Al-Monitor. Additionally, his papers have been accepted for international conferences of the International Studies Association, International Society of Iranian Studies, and the International Organization for Conflict Management. He also has a BS in Management and worked for a decade as a journalist and chief editor. item.description:
Amelia Malpas

PhD student, Department of Government, GSAS
email amalpas@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Amelia Malpas is a PhD student in Government studying American and comparative politics, with a broad interest in the relationship between politics and the distribution of material resources in society. Her primary research agenda to address how this topic concerns political parties’ and intraparty factions’ policy programs exacerbate, maintain, or ameliorate socioeconomic inequalities. Put most directly, she studies the political economy of parties with special attention to factional conflict within party coalitions over distributional issues.

item.description:
Aljoša Milenković

PhD Candidate in Linguistics, Graduate School of Arts & Sciences
email aljosamilenkovic@g.harvard.edu

Aljoša Milenković is a PhD candidate in Linguistics at Harvard University with research interests in phonology, historical linguistics, metrics, syntax, computational and corpus linguistics. Aljoša uses corpora and experimental methods to study natural language sound patterns, sentence structure, and poetic meter. His ongoing projects combine Maximum Entropy models with machine learning techniques to analyze and model phonological variation.

item.title: Alina Herderich
item.description:
Alina Herderich

Visiting Fellow, Digital Emotions Lab, D^3/LISH
PhD student, Graz University of Technology
email alinaherderich@fas.harvard.edu
laptop_windows GitHub
  • Twitter

Alina Herderich is a predoctoral researcher at the Digital Emotions Lab led by Amit Goldenberg at HBS. Her home university is the Graz University of Technology. The majority of her undergraduate and graduate studies comprised psychology, which she complemented with a substantial amount of computer science. Since then she enjoys being a Computational Social Scientist who uses data science tools, especially from natural language processing, to shed new light on old and newly arising psychological questions. Her fields of interest are manifold, but she mainly focuses on emotion regulation theory and measurement (e.g., building a data-driven taxonomy of emotion regulation strategies) and collective moderation of public online spaces (e.g., citizen counter speech on social media platforms). Visit her website to find out more.

item.title: Alice Xu
item.description:
Alice Xu

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email alicexu@g.harvard.edu

Alice Xu is a Ph.D. Candidate in the Department of Government at Harvard, focusing on the comparative political economy of inequality/welfare politics as well as on urban and environmental development in Latin America and East Africa. Prior to starting her Ph.D., she worked in the energy and environmental markets consulting industry and received her B.A. in Economics-Political Science and Sustainable Development from Columbia University.

item.title: Alice Sommer
item.description:
Alice Sommer

Ludwig-Maximilian University (LMU) of Munich
Alice is a PhD candidate in Human Biology at the Ludwig-Maximilian University (LMU) of Munich. At Harvard, she is an Institute Fellow working in Marie-Abèle Bind’s Lab at the Department of Statistics of FAS. She completed an MSc in Statistics at the KU Leuven in Belgium. Her research interests focus on applying causal inference methods to understand environmental epidemiology problems. Her projects include the estimation of effects of air pollution on multiple sclerosis relapses and on the human gut microbiome.

item.title: Alexander Klapheke
item.description:
Alexander Klapheke

Linguistics, GSAS
email My research is in the semantics of natural language: how utterances come to have the meanings they do, and how meaning interacts with linguistic structure. Currently I am studying how the behavior of polarity items—words like ever which can only appear i

My research is in the semantics of natural language: how utterances come to have the meanings they do, and how meaning interacts with linguistic structure. Currently I am studying how the behavior of polarity items—words like ever which can only appear in certain places, like following before but not after—differs between English and other languages, and am running online experiments to gather data and test hypotheses about these differences.

item.title: Aleksei Opacic
item.description:
Aleksei Opacic

PhD Candidate in Sociology, GSAS
email aopacic@g.harvard.edu

Aleksei Opacic is a PhD candidate in Sociology. Most broadly, Aleksei is interested in the intergenerational transmission of (dis)advantage and the causal mechanisms underpinning this transmission. In particular, his research aims to integrate causal perspectives on the role of schooling and education in socioeconomic attainment with the more descriptive tradition that has often characterized social mobility research. Before starting at Harvard, Aleksei worked as a policy researcher at a London-based think tank. He obtained a BA in Social and Political Science from Cambridge and an MSc in Sociology from Oxford.

item.title: Alejandro Lagomarsino
item.description:
Alejandro Lagomarsino

Economics, GSAS
email alagomarsino@g.harvard.edu
Alejandro LagomarsiNo is a third-year PhD student at the Department of Economics. His research interests are in labor ecoNomics, political ecoNomy and development ecoNomics with an emphasis on applied work on middle-income countries, and Non-reduced form analysis. He is currently modelling and estimating how educational decisions in rural Latin America are affected by changes in commodity prices. Prior to grad school, Alejandro worked at the Center for the Study of EcoNomic and Social Affairs (think tank) where he was responsible for the Uruguayan Micro-Development division.

item.description:
Adrienne Sabety

Health Policy, Economics Track, GSAS
email asabety@g.harvard.edu
Adrienne's research focuses on how to produce high quality health care in an office-based setting. Her main work asks how important the patient-provider relationship is to the quality of care. She is also working with New York City's Department of Mental Health and Hygiene to design and evaluate a health care program for undocumented immigrants, which is piloted as a randomized control trial.

item.title: Adrian Scheibler
item.description:
Adrian Scheibler

PhD Student, Department of Government, GSAS
email ascheibler@fas.harvard.edu

Adrian Scheibler is a Ph.D. student in the Harvard Department of Government. He is interested in the areas of political culture, political behavior, and historical and comparative political economy. His main focus centers on the interaction between social cleavages and institutional design and development in advanced democracies.

item.title: Abtsam Saleh
item.description:
Abtsam Saleh

PhD Candidate in CSR, GSAS
email asaleh@g.harvard.edu

Abtsam is a PhD candidate in the Study of Religion with a Data Science secondary field. Her research examines questions surrounding human-computer interaction and community formation in transregional perspectives. Through her research and fellowships, she has explored a range of issues related to human computer interaction, surveillance, the ethical use of AI, digital infrastructure, and digital humanities methods.

item.title: Aaron Watanabe
item.description:
Aaron Watanabe

PhD Candidate, Department of Government, FAS
email awatanabe@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Aaron is a PhD student in Harvard's Department of Government. His research in comparative politics focuses on political regimes, populism, and elites, especially in Latin America and Europe. He is currently working on a dissertation about the causes of divisions among so-called "establishment" elites and their implications for politics.

item.title: Aakaash Rao
item.description:
Aakaash Rao

PhD Candidate, Department of Economics
email arao@g.harvard.edu

Aakaash Rao studies social norms and social learning, with a particular interest in the role of ideology and political identity. In current work, he is examining the role of "excuses" in normalizing public expression of stigmatized views, the consequences of political ideology for the success of public programs, social dynamics among Chinese college students in the United States, and "groupthink" and political mobilization in India.

item.title: Yuting Chen
item.description:
Yuting Chen

Visiting fellow, Center for Geographic Analysis
email yutingchen@fas.harvard.edu

Yuting Chen is a PhD student from the Institute of Space and Earth Information Science, the Chinese University of Hong Kong. Her research primarily revolves around virtual geographic environment, geographic knowledge engineering and geospatial big data analysis. She has a background of computer science and software engineering. During her PhD study, she worked on the project “Collaborative Simulation and Evaluation of Interaction between Global Change and Human Activities and Environment Based on Virtual Geographic Environment”, concerned with intelligent data and model sharing and multi-dimensional visualization for collaborative geographic research. As a visit scholar of CGA, she will study under the supervision of Prof. Jason Ur and Dr. Wendy Guan.

item.title: Yusuf Celik
item.description:
Yusuf Celik

Postdoctoral Fellow, Program in Islamic Law, Harvard Law School
email yusufcelik@icloud.com

Dr. Yusuf Celik's research is on philosophical hermeneutics, posthumanism, and the Digital Humanities. Dr. Celik has also been active for years in the field of software engineering. As an independent contractor, he has worked for different high-profile clients in the capacity of lead developer, consultant, code coach, and Scrum master. He is currently architecting and developing a variety of tools and platforms that allow researchers, including himself, to derive quantifiable insights from historical and legal texts.

item.title: Yuqi Chen

Yuqi Chen

laptop_windows Website

Yuqi is working on the China Biographical Database (CBDB) Project at IQSS. Her research interests are in the fields of Quantitative History and Digital Humanities, with a particular focus on Social Network Analysis of archaeological data.

item.title: Victoria S Asbury
item.description:
Victoria S Asbury

PhD Candidate, Department of Sociology at Harvard University
email vasbury@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Victoria S. Asbury is a PhD Candidate in Sociology at Harvard University studying political behavior and attitudes. She draws upon computational text analyses, conjoint experiments, surveys, and behavioral economic games to investigate the content and effects of elite political discourse predicated on appeals to American identity. She was a 2019 Summer Institute in Computational Social Sciences resident fellow at Princeton University.

item.title: Thiri Shwesin Aung
item.description:
Thiri Shwesin Aung

Research Associate, TH Chan School of Public Health
laptop_windows Conflict and Environmental Forensics Working Group (CEFWG)

Thiri is an accomplished environmental scientist specializing in Land Cover and Land-Use Change (LCLUC) and energy systems. Her extensive expertise includes remote sensing, ecology, biogeochemistry, and land change modeling. Currently, Thiri's research focuses on advancing methods, models, and emerging technologies in data science for processing, analyzing, and fusing heterogeneous remote sensing data, and their applications to Earth System Science and areas of societal benefit, particularly in data-scarce conflict regions. Thiri's approach involves using geospatial science and data to capture, analyze, and predict the intricacies of LCLUC at various scales, allowing for an understanding of the environmental and societal impacts of such changes through land system analysis. She has extensive experience investigating the impacts of armed conflict on land use, cropland abandonment, and socioeconomics, utilizing remote sensing, geospatial analysis, and modeling approaches. Her work has been published in over 20 peer-reviewed scientific journals. Thiri leads the CEFWG, an organization that utilizes geography and remote sensing to establish connections between environmental condition, land use, and climate change with processes of conflict, displacement, and resilience. Her research has received funding from esteemed institutions such as the European Union, the World Bank, and the Norwegian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Thiri is currently focused on developing innovative methods to harness satellite data and artificial intelligence to investigate human rights violations and environmental crimes during conflict and resource exploration.

item.title: Sophie Weston Chien
item.description:
Sophie Weston Chien

Project Associate, Climate Justice Design Fellowship
Graduate Student, AP/MUP, Graduate School of Design
email schien@gsd.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter

Sophie Weston Chien is a designer-organizer. She builds liberated spaces and communities through social and ecological design. This means altering power in society through the built environment and designing resilient relationships with care. Her interdisciplinary practice takes the form of architecture, landscape architecture, urban planning, writing, textile, exhibition, and graphic design, as well as teaching and organizing within the design field. She is currently a Project Associate for the Climate Justice Design Fellowship.

item.title: Sima Biondi
item.description:
Sima Biondi

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email sbiondi@g.harvard.edu

Sima Biondi is a PhD student in the Government Department at Harvard. She studies comparative political economy and political development, with a particular focus on colonialism and historical state building. Before coming to Harvard, she worked as a researcher for the University of Chicago's Law and Economics group and earned a BA in Political Science with a minor in Mathematical and Computational Sciences at Stanford.

item.title: Shweta Bhogale
item.description:
Shweta Bhogale

PhD Candidate, Public Policy
email sbhogale@g.harvard.edu

Shweta's research interests are at the intersection of development economics, political economy, and environmental resources. She currently works on research about geographical and administrative determinants of water use and crop choice in rural India, in addition to projects about political entry , politician incentives, and electoral reforms for municipalities in urban India. She has benefited greatly from IQSS resources on learning python and web-scraping.

item.title: Shiyin Chen
item.description:
Shiyin Chen

Visiting Scholar, China Biographical Database
email chenshiyin007@126.com
laptop_windows Website

He majors in Confucian Classics and Ming history. In recent years, he has published some books about Confucian Classics study and Ming history, namely, Annotations about The Book of Rites (Fudan University Press, 2018), Supplementary Remarks of Chen Hao’s Explanation about The Book of Rites (Anhui Education Press, 2020), From Duke Zhou to First Emperor of Qin: Autobiographies of 12 famous people (Lijiang Press, 2019)，Unstable Identity: A Brief History of Ming Dynasty Based-on Ritual System (Zhejiang Guji Press, 2021).

item.title: Shelby Carvalho
item.description:
Shelby Carvalho

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email scarvalho1@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows Twitter

Shelby is an advanced doctoral candidate, Presidential Merit Fellow, and Fulbright recipient in Government. Her work focuses broadly on the political economy of education and employment and on migration and forced displacement in low- and middle-income countries with a regional focus on sub-Saharan Africa. Outside of Harvard, Shelby regularly works with international organizations including the World Bank and UNHCR. Shelby began her career in education as a teacher. Following her time in the classroom, she spent five years working with the World Bank’s Africa Gender Innovation Lab and Education Global Practice on projects throughout southern Africa, Ethiopia, and Mexico. Shelby teaches courses on international aid to education and in applied statistics at HGSE. She holds master’s degrees in Global Policy Studies from the UT Austin LBJ School of Public Affairs and in Education Policy from Harvard Graduate School of Education, and a BA in Government from The University of Texas.

item.title: Shani Cohen
item.description:
Shani Cohen

PhD Candidate in Economics, GSAS
email shani_cohen@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Shani is an economics PhD candidate at Harvard, studying economic theory and macroeconomics, with the intersection of behavioral economics.

item.title: Sean Bock
item.description:
Sean Bock

Graduate student, Department of Sociology
I am a PhD student in the Department of Sociology at Harvard University. My research uses quantitative approaches to study topics broadly related to political culture and social change in the U.S. and Western European contexts. I'm particularly interested in the development and consequences of Neo-Conservative politics since the 1980's.

item.title: Sarah Duncan
item.description:
Sarah Duncan

IT Operations Manager, IQSS/Harvard-MIT Data Center
email slduncan@fas.harvard.edu

Sarah Duncan is thrilled to be working at IQSS supporting Social Science research using Linux.  Sarah has an undergraduate degree in Engineering and Applied Science, and a Masters in Divinity, both from Harvard, so this job satisfies both aspects of her interests.  Sarah has worked at Harvard before, as webweaver for the Department of Genetics at the Medical School, and for eleven and a half years as Jill of all IT trades for the Harrison/Wiley x-ray crystallography labs in the Biochemistry department.  Most recently, Sarah worked for eight years at WGBH as a UNIX, Storage, and Windows Systems Administrator.

item.title: Sarah Baum
item.description:
Sarah Baum

PhD Candidate, Department of Global Health and Population, HSPH
email sbaum@g.harvard.edu

Sarah Baum is a PhD candidate in the Department of Global Health and Population at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. She studies drivers of patient and provider demand for diagnostic information, interventions aimed at improving the allocation of testing, and approaches to correct potential biases in surveillance data. Her research combines applied microeconomics, infectious disease modeling, and quasi-experimental causal inference methods.

Raúl Duarte is a Ph.D. student in Political Economy and Government. His research interests span political economy, economic development, comparative politics, and public finance. Among his specific interests are public service delivery, state capacity, social networks, and clientelism.

Before his doctoral studies, he graduated from Harvard Kennedy School's MPA/ID program and from Washington University in St. Louis where he studied Economics and Mathematics.

item.title: Philip Brookins
item.description:
Philip Brookins

Visiting Fellow, Laboratory for Innovation Science at Harvard
email brookins@coll.mpg.de
laptop_windows Website
laptop_windows LISH

Philip Brookins is a senior research fellow at the Max Planck Institute for Research on Collective Goods in Bonn, Germany. His research involves the use of game-theoretic techniques to model and characterize behavior in competitive settings, such as contests and tournaments, and the use of experimental methods to test the validity of such models. In particular, he examines how individuals and groups behave in contests when they are information-constrained, e.g., Not fully informed as to the number of competitors and/or their characteristics, as well as how they behave when competing as part of a team composed of heterogeneously talented members. Philip joined the Max Planck Institute in 2016 after receiving a doctoral, master's, and bachelor's degree in ecoNomics and a bachelor's degree in mathematics from Florida State University.

item.title: Omar Barbiero
item.description:
Omar Barbiero

Economics, GSAS
email obarbiero@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Omar Barbiero is a Ph.D. candidate in Economics at Harvard University. He uses both empirical and theoretical methods to study the economic effects of exchange rate exposures, fiscal policies, and trade policies. His current research explores how foreign-priced operations generate currency mismatch exposure on balance sheet of firms. He earned a BSc in Statistics at the University of Padua in 2010, and a MSc in Economics at Bocconi University in 2013.

item.title: Oliver Bertuch
item.description:
Oliver Bertuch

Developer, Dataverse Core Development Team
Central Library, Forschungszentrum Jülich
Co-PI, HERMES Project
email o.bertuch@fz-juelich.de
laptop_windows Website

Oliver is a Research Software Engineer working on solutions for management, publication and archiving of research data and research software. His mission is bringing together these two worlds, fostering Open Science and Reproducibility. Located at the Central Library of Forschungszentrum Jülich, Germany, he works closely together with the IQSS as part of the Dataverse Core Team. His focus there is addressing topics like containerization and research software readiness of Dataverse repositories. As Co-PI of HERMES he develops a new key infrastructure element for automated, metadata rich research software publications.

item.title: Nour
item.description:
Nour-Lyna Boulgamh

Doctoral Candidate in Design: Urban Economics and Real Estate, Graduate School of Design
email nour_lyna@gsd.harvard.edu

Nour-Lyna Boulgamh is an architect and a doctoral candidate specializing in urban economics and housing affordability. She holds a BSc in Architectural Engineering and a Master’s in Design Studies from Harvard University’s Graduate School of Design. Her research focuses on the intersection of urban economics and affordable housing, with a particular emphasis on the use of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) tools and quantitative methods to analyze urban environments and housing markets.

item.title: Noah Dasanaike
item.description:
Noah Dasanaike

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email noahdasanaike@g.harvard.edu

Noah is a PhD student in the Department of Government interested in geography and behavior, particularly in a comparative and historical context (persistence, legacies, historical political economy). More specifically, he is interested in how geography influences political behavior, and how political behavior in turn varies across space. He is also interested in the development and application of statistical methods for studying these topics.

item.title: Noah Daponte
item.description:
Noah Daponte-Smith

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email dapontesmith@g.harvard.edu

Noah Daponte-Smith studies comparative politics and the political economy of advanced democracies, with a focus on party systems, redistribution and inequality, and regional divides.

item.title: Nicole Alexander
item.description:
Nicole Alexander

Visiting Researcher, Center for Geographic Analysis
email nalexander@cga.harvard.edu

Nicole is a Visiting Researcher at the CGA and was previously a Principal Member of Technical Staff on the Oracle Spatial development team. Nicole has a Ph.D. in Geography from the University of California, Santa Barbara, a M.S. in Geographic and Geodetic Information Systems from the University of London, and a B.S. in Land Surveying from the University of the West Indies. Prior to working for Oracle Corporation, Nicole’s experience in the field of geomatics included, a data capture engineer/GIS specialist at Trimble, a GIS consultant at Fujitsu-ICL, and a land surveyor on the Channel Tunnel project linking England to France.

item.title: Nicholas Conroy
item.description:
Nicholas Conroy

Nicholas is a PhD student in the Government Department at Harvard University. His research focuses on Congress, inequality, and representation, including the class backgrounds of federal legislators. Before starting his PhD, Nicholas worked as a staffer in the US House of Representatives.

Her research interests include developing and applying computational methods, including geospatial analytics, machine learning, and modeling, towards questions of conflict and security. Prior to Harvard, she worked in data consulting before earning her MS in Computational Analysis and Public Policy at the University of Chicago.

Mehmet Durmaz is a master's student at the Center for Middle Eastern Studies. Before joining Harvard, he completed his undergraduate studies in Economics and History at Boğaziçi University. Mehmet's research interests include understanding how political-economic institutions mediate between economic changes and political outcomes during democratic backsliding, as well as the relationship between these institutions and specific regime outcomes.

PhD Student, Department of Government

item.title: Matthew E Dardet
item.description:
Matthew E Dardet

PhD Candidate in Government, GSAS
email matthewdardet@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Website

Matthew Dardet's research integrates methodology, public opinion, and psychology to more precisely investigate political phenomena within the American context. His latest work involves formally quantifying uncertainty in predicting electoral outcomes, assessing the impacts of the Internet and social media on voting behavior and campaign finance, and improving survey data quality using questionnaire design, experiments, and machine learning.

item.title: Marissa A Combs
item.description:
Marissa A Combs

PhD Student in Sociology, FAS
PIER Fellow, Center for Education Policy Research at Harvard University
email marissacombs@g.harvard.edu

Marissa Combs is a PhD Student in Sociology, a PIER Fellow at the Center for Education Policy Research, and a Presidential Scholar in the Graduate School of Arts and Sciences. Her research sits at the intersection of sociology of education, organizations, culture, and economic sociology. She is interested in the contours and consequences of market-based accountability in K-12 public schools and the organizational factors shaping public education reform in the US.

item.title: María Ballesteros
item.description:
María Ballesteros

PhD Student at the Department of Government
Graduate School of Arts and Sciences Prize Fellow
email mballesteros@g.harvard.edu
laptop_windows Twitter

Maria Ballesteros is a PhD Student in Government at Harvard University using quantitative methods to study the role of gender in political violence. Originally from Mexico City, María graduated with honors from ITAM with a B.A. in Political Science. Prior to coming to Harvard, María worked as a research assistant at the Harris School of Public Policy, at The University of Chicago, researching the political economy of conflicts in Colombia, Chile and Afghanistan.

Leopoldo's research interests include international institutions, international trade and investment, the European Union, and Japan. His work focuses on the institutional design of international trade and investment agreements, and more recently on on treaty implementation.
